The average standardized uptake value (SUV) bias was 0.013 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.032 SUV for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-CFT and -0.175 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.149 SUV for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-DOPA. The proposed cross-tracer PET synthesis model achieves comparable performance with <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-CFT or <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-DOPA PET imaging for PD studies.

title: Tactile Sensing for Wheeled Robot Navigation in Open Worlds: A Simple yet Robust Approach
authors: Florian Ehrlich-Sommer, Christof Müller, Bernhard Kager, Bernhard Hörl, Jean-Baptiste Weibel, Andreas Holzinger
abstract: Autonomous ground robots are moving from structured indoor settings to complex real-world environments where sensing is unreliable and contact is unavoidable: forests and shrublands, construction sites, warehouses with dense clutter, mines and tunnels, and post-disaster debris fields. In such domains, standard navigation stacks break when occlusion, deformable obstacles, dust/rain, and intermittent global localization violate clean perception assumptions. We argue that the right question is not how to avoid all contact, but how to make safe, low-speed contact informative. This paper proposes to embrace contact with tactile sensing to improve navigation rather than consider them as failures. A segmented compliant bumper provides coarse contact localization and a force proxy. Each contact event is time-aligned with LiDAR and camera streams and encoded as a structured interaction constraint that can support near-field mapping, traversability reasoning, and contact-aware local decisions when perception is ambiguous. On the engineering side, we provide a reproducible CAD-to-FEA workflow to compare bumper geometries and select a segmented W-profile that yields scenario-dependent strain hotspots, as well as mechanical compliance enabling both horizontal segment attribution and vertical interaction discrimination. A strain-gauge prototype segment validates repeatable bridge-response patterns across frontal and gliding/overriding loads. In simulation (Gazebo/ROS 2) with a cluttered forest stand generated from real 3D inventory data, leveraging these tactile events enables even a minimal reactive policy to maintain progress and produce interpretable collision clusters that highlight constrained regions. Across 30 simulation runs of 10 min each, the reactive tactile policy achieved a mean area coverage of 38.19% (±6.1 pp standard deviation) of a 40×40m forest stand, demonstrating sustained exploration progress despite frequent contact. Beyond immediate robustness, the logged tactile events and time-aligned sensor streams provide a structured dataset that can enable future self-supervised learning of traversability predictors from pre-contact context.

title: Towards Wearable Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition: A Survey
authors: Hailin Zou, Zijie Chen, Yuanyuan Pan, Jianqing Li
abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR), which aims to identify and classify human behaviors through multi-source sensing data, has become a long-standing research hotspot in ubiquitous computing, enabling a wide range of real-world applications. Focusing on Wearable HAR (WHAR), this survey provides a system-level synthesis organized around the life cycle of WHAR systems, collating fundamental theories, key technologies, and implementation details across data acquisition, model construction, and system deployment. Rather than treating sensing configuration, learning paradigm selection, evaluation protocol design, and deployment constraints as isolated topics, we emphasize their inherent interdependencies, demonstrating how early design choices propagate through the entire development pipeline to shape final system performance. We further establish a unified taxonomy of HAR paradigms based on data collection devices, clarifying the boundaries between vision-based, ambient sensor-based, and wearable sensor-based approaches to eliminate long-standing classification ambiguities. Additionally, we systematically review mainstream supervised WHAR models, emerging learning paradigms, deployment-oriented evaluation criteria, and cutting-edge directions including foundation models and Large Language Model (LLM)-based activity understanding. By integrating all aspects into a coherent life cycle framework, this survey aims to serve as a design-oriented reference for researchers and practitioners building accurate, efficient, and deployable WHAR systems.

title: A Closed Loop Model Predictive Control for Beamforming in RIS-aided Communications
authors: Luis Carlos Mathias, Rafael Marasca Martins, Taufik Abrão
abstract: One of the main challenges in implementing reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) is the latency between changes in channel conditions and the corresponding system response, as well as addressing this issue through the establishment of an electromagnetic feedback signal that enables closed-loop (CL) control. Given this scenario, this work proposes a beamforming control scheme based on non-linear model predictive control (MPC), which is capable of proactively compensating for accumulated delays and optimizing the received power at a mobile user equipment (UE). In downlink (DL), the predictive model uses the phase and power of the received signal as feedback through an extended Kalman filter (EKF), enabling a viable CL control strategy for a RIS-aided wireless communication setup. Numerical results involving joint angular/radial velocity inversion of the UE confirm the effectiveness of the proposed MPC in compensating for the RIS control setup delays, while maintaining near-optimal received power.

title: Heterogeneous Graph-Based Partner Recommendation for EU Framework Programmes
authors: Jeovane Honorio Alves, Stefan Morcov, Radu State
abstract: Assembling competitive consortia for EU Framework Programme proposals requires identifying partners that are thematically relevant, institutionally compatible, and programmatically aligned — yet partner search typically defaults to existing networks, narrowing the search space. We formulate seeded partner recommendation — conditioned on a target project and one known consortium member — as a ranking problem over a heterogeneous knowledge graph constructed from CORDIS data (FP1 through Horizon Europe), comprising around 504K nodes and 1.55M typed edges. The model integrates collaboration history, thematic topics, legal and funding context, geography, and text embeddings, and is evaluated under time-aware splits simulating forward-looking recommendation. Among seven GNN operators, SAGEConv achieves MRR of 35.04% and Hits@10 of 62.43% in warm-start, and MRR of 33.56% and Hits@10 of 61.39% in cold-start. Ablation studies show that legal basis, funding scheme, and project-side semantics are the dominant signals for recommendation quality.

title: Automated Denoising of Mammographic Microcalcifications Using Wavelet-Based Attention Deep Network for the Medical Internet of Things
authors: Khalil ur Rehman, Anaa Yasin, Li Jianqiang, Ayesha Jabbar, Yibin Tian
abstract: Breast microcalcifications are key radiological indicators of malignancy, and their assessment in denser breast tissues is challenging. We propose a Wavelet-based Attention Deep Network (WADN) that isolates high-frequency components linked to microcalcification clusters using denoising wavelet transforms and integrates them into a deep attention architecture for interpretable, localization-assisted classification. The system is deployed in an edge-computing framework enabled by Jetson Orin Nano to support real-time analysis and remote radiologist access for the Medical Internet of Things (MIoT). For the private PINUM and public DDSM datasets, WADN outperforms standard convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and classical machine learning baselines, achieving accuracies of 0.95 and 0.93, respectively, with consistent gains in sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and area under the curve (AUC). On the IoT edge device, the method achieves accuracies of 0.91 for PINUM and 0.93 for DDSM, demonstrating the feasibility of ondevice screening. These results indicate that combining wavelet transforms with attention mechanisms and IoT edge deployment improves diagnostic accuracy while enabling practical real-time breast-cancer screening workflows.

title: Monitoring Agricultural Transformation and Its Impacts on Water Resources in the Gharb Plain, Morocco, Using Machine Learning and Remote Sensing
authors: Tarik Bahouq, Adil Moumane, Abdessamad Elmotawakkil, Nadia Touhami, Suraj Kumar Bhagat
abstract: Driven by national initiatives like the Green Morocco Plan, Morocco’s Gharb Plain has shifted from traditional rain-fed farming to intensive, high-value commercial cultivation. Previous regional research has documented land-use changes and groundwater stress separately.Acritical research gap remains in capturing the direct, seasonal interactions between long-term surface greening and subsurface aquifer degradation, where apparent vegetation expansion often masks underlying resource depletion. This study aims to bridge this gap by quantifying the direct relationship between long-term agricultural intensification and localized hydrological stress, within a representative study area of the Gharb Plain.We adopted an integrated, multi-scale approach combining four decades (1984–2024) of Landsat satellite imagery processed via Google Earth Engine (GEE) with seasonal field observations. A Random Forest machine-learning algorithm and spectral indices tracked land use/land cover (LULC) changes. These findings were cross-validated with in situ seasonal hydrochemical monitoring across 68 wells spanning four distinct soil-agricultural zones. Long-term data confirmed robust vegetation densification, with EVI increasing by 1.24 km2/year due to perennial avocado expansion. Seasonal LULC mapping showed the agricultural footprint peaks in winter (166.5 km2) and contracts to its minimum in summer (113.7 km2). This summer contraction marks peak hydrological stress: intensive irrigation pumping caused electrical conductivity (EC) to spike to 2,909 μS/cm in the sandy loam zone and 2,888 μS/cm in the sandy-clay zone, whilewater table depths dropped to seasonal lows of 6.75 m and 7 m, respectively. This work establishes a vital baseline reference. Practically, these insights enable regional water management authorities and policy planners to deploy targeted multi-year monitoring, implement water-efficient irrigation technologies, and restrict water-intensive crops to protect the plain’s long-term agricultural viability.

title: Enhancing Stability and Transient Response of LLC Resonant Converters Using a Reduced-Order Model with Pole-Zero Cancellation
authors: Dohong Lee, Bonggook Kim, Younghoon Cho
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: LLC resonant converters are widely adopted for their high efficiency, but their control design poses significant challenges due to the complex beat-frequency double pole dynamics that shift with operating conditions. Conventional control methods often restrict bandwidth to ensure stability, resulting in poor transient responses. This paper proposes a robust pole-canceling compensator to achieve both high control bandwidth and fast transient response. First, a reduced-order second-order small-signal model is derived. Based on this model, a pole-canceling compensator is synthesized to effectively eliminate the resonant peak, enabling a potentially higher crossover frequency with sufficient stability margins. The proposed control strategy offers a systematic design methodology that yields a practical low-order controller, facilitating straightforward implementation without computational burdens. The proposed method is compared with a conventional design, demonstrating superior dynamic performance and robustness against the parameter variations. Experimental results on a 500-W prototype confirm that the proposed scheme significantly reduces settling time and eliminates high-frequency ringing compared to conventional designs.

title: Passive Broadband Achromaticity in Fiber-Integrated Multifocal Metalens via Flat-Top Energy Window
authors: Peihua Wangyang, Jiangqing Xu, Jiamin Zhang, Yanrong Deng, Gongli Xiao, Junqi Chen
abstract: Metalenses offer a compact platform for wavefront manipulation but suffer from severe chromatic dispersion in broadband fiber-integrated applications. We propose an ultra-compact multifocal metalens monolithically integrated on an optical fiber facet, achieving broadband dispersion tolerance from 640 to 740 nm. Instead of relying on complex dispersion-engineered meta-atoms, we construct a flat-top axial energy window to passively accommodate wavelength-dependent focal shifts. This mechanism is realized through a precision-equalized three-dimensional Gerchberg–Saxton (GS) framework enabling axial energy redistribution. The device generates four highly uniform focal spots with an extended depth of focus, maintaining stable focal intensity profiles across the entire bandwidth, with an effective depth of focus (EDOF) spanning 7.6–10.3 μm (~4.0–6.2× the diffraction limit). Simulation results show an average focusing efficiency of ~60% with a peak of 63.83% at 680 nm, while the focal spot full width at half maximum (FWHM) varies between 852 and 992 nm. Featuring high coupling efficiency and strict channel isolation (crosstalk < -25 dB), this compact architecture directly overcomes core bottlenecks in broadband multi-channel fiber coupling, providing a highly scalable solution for high-capacity space-division multiplexing (SDM) systems.

title: Knowledge Graph-based Debiasing for Trustworthy Recommendation Systems
authors: Youru Li, Xuying Ning, Zhenfeng Zhu, Hanqiu Wang, Zhi Cai, Minnan Luo, Qinghua Zheng, Yao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: These years have witnessed remarkable progress in modeling user behaviour from personalized online services, especially knowledge graph-based recommendation systems. Meanwhile, more studies are focusing on aspects beyond recommendation performance, since such an observational data-driven paradigm is posing threats to both users and society in terms of trustworthiness. In fact, existing problem-oriented solutions still face significant challenges, as almost all of them suffer from the generality limitations to improve their trustworthiness in a uniform fashion. To address these issues, we propose a plug-and-play <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">D</u>eb<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</u>asing framework for <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">K</u>nowledge <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">G</u>raph-based <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>ecommendation <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u>ystems, also known as DiKGRS. Specifically, the Knowledge-augmented Pseudo-Samples Generation (KPSG) method, a novel data augmentation perspective, is proposed to explore more auxiliary information beyond observational user behaviors. Furthermore, the Debiasing Value Networks (DVN), is also developed to evaluate the reliability of generated pseudo-samples by modeling both the item popularity and user demographic bias in the platform. Moreover, an adaptive weighting coordination module is performed to coordinate the proposed DiKGRS framework and its backbones. Experimental results on four real-world datasets from different online service personalization scenarios have illustrated that the proposed framework can significantly improve the trustworthiness of existing knowledge graph-based recommendation systems. The code has been released public available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/alipay/A-Knowledge-augmented-Method-DiKGRS</uri>.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of FAS-ARIS Communications for 6G: System Modeling and Analytical Insights
authors: Hong-Bae Jeon, Kai-Kit Wong, Chan-Byoung Chae
abstract: This paper introduces a unified analytical and optimization framework for fluid antenna system-active reconfigurable intelligent surface (FAS-ARIS) communications in 6G. By combining the port reconfigurability of FAS with the signal amplification of ARIS, the proposed design enables more flexible control of the propagation environment and enhanced link reliability beyond what passive solutions can offer. We first derive the optimal ARIS amplification gain under a reflection power constraint to maximize the user’s signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Using a block-diagonal matrix approximation, we obtain a tractable outage expression and a tight independent-antenna equivalent upper-bound. Building on this, we establish the monotonic relationship between outage and effective channel gain, which enables a closed-form solution for ARIS phase optimization under limited channel state information (CSI). To further improve spectral efficiency, we propose a region-partitioned throughput optimization framework that achieves near-optimal performance without exhaustive search, thereby verifying its low computational complexity. Extensive simulations confirm the accuracy of the analysis and demonstrate consistent gains in outage and throughput compared to baselines.

title:
Extremum Seeking With Double Integrators in the Presence of Local Extrema
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Raik Suttner, Christian Ebenbauer, Sergey Dashkovskiy
abstract: We study the problem of global extremum seeking in the presence of local extrema. We investigate two different perturbation-based methods: 1) a well-known classical extremum seeking scheme for steady-state output optimization, and 2) a source seeking scheme for a two-dimensional point mass. In each of these two scenarios, the closed-loop system involves a damped double integrator subject to an oscillatory force. An averaging analysis reveals that the respective averaged system is again a damped double integrator, but now subject to a potential force. The potential force is given by the gradient of a locally averaged objective function. Such a function is less prone to have undesired local extrema and is therefore better suited for global optimization. We provide sufficient conditions for semi-global practical uniform asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system. The sufficient conditions only involve assumptions on the averaged objective function but not the original one.

title: Towards Sustainable Smart Cities with AGI-Enabled Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking Systems: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Jifar Wakuma Ayana, Zita Lifelo, Huansheng Ning, Jianguo Ding
abstract: Sustainable smart cities require cross-domain, adaptive, and anticipatory intelligence. Current Internet of Things and Artificial Intelligence systems are largely siloed, limiting their ability to support comprehensive urban management. This review addresses this gap by examining the prospective integration of Artificial General Intelligence with the Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking paradigm. We synthesize research published from January 2015 to September 2025 to provide an overview of how Artificial General Intelligence can serve as the cognitive core, facilitating proactive orchestration across Internet of Things-driven urban layers. Specifically, the paper (1) presents a conceptual analysis of reasoning and meta-cognitive capabilities within the Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking framework, (2) maps enabling technologies, including digital twins, neuro-symbolic Artificial Intelligence, and the edge-cloud continuum, that operationalize this vision, and (3) reviews applications in mobility, energy management, and climate resilience, highlighting potential gains in efficiency, adaptability, and sustainability. Key technical, ethical, and governance challenges, such as scalability, security, and algorithmic fairness, are discussed. By integrating these insights, the review provides a unified perspective and a roadmap for future research on human-centric, resilient, and sustainable urban intelligent systems.

title: Referral Required: When AI Blocks Access to Human Doctors

title: Large Language Model Driven Clinical Knowledge Graph Enhancing Medical Decision Accuracy
authors: Abhishek Gokula
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The growing complexity of clinical decisionmaking in oncology, especially in lung cancer multidisciplinary team consultations, can be explained by the simultaneous presence of heterogeneous data silos and ontological burden hindering timely and accurate diagnoses and therapeutics. Current methodologies that rely solely on large language models are riddled with hallucinations and fail to explain interpretability, while static knowledge graphs lack the flexibility to include changing evidence. In response, the present work proposes LLM- ClinKG, a hybrid framework, which dynamically augments clinical knowledge graphs through entity-relation extraction and multimodal fusion performed by large language models followed by the symbolic-inductive reasoning for link prediction and counter factual analysis. Evaluation on multimodal lung cancer cohort results show that the system achieves F1 score of 92% in relation extraction (14 % increment improvement compared to baseline methods), 0.89 area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve for relapse risk prediction and 45 % consultation time reduction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 5 \%}$</tex> data utilisation. LLM-ClinKG thus promotes the field of trustworthy artificial intelligence by providing interpretable evidence-based help in improving diagnostic accuracy and efficiency of work flows in high-stakes oncology.

title: Improved CBBA for Multi-AUV Task Allocation Under Weak Acoustic Communication Using a Five-Regime Evaluation with 6-DOF Dynamics
authors: Hailin Wang, Shuo Li, Yiqun Wang, Tianyou Qiu, Yiping Li
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: Cooperative task allocation for multiple autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) is commonly studied under ideal communication assumptions, which seldom hold in underwater acoustic networks. This paper presents an improved ConsensusBased Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) with three main contributions: (i) a communication-robust consensus update that incorporates local conflict resolution, deterministic tie-breaking, and local bundle repair; (ii) a marginal cost model informed by six-degree-offreedom (6-DOF) dynamics that accounts for turning and depthtransition feasibility during task insertion; and (iii) a five-regime evaluation framework spanning IDEAL, GOOD, MODERATE, LIMITED, and SEVERE acoustic communication conditions. The method is evaluated in an integrated simulation environment that couples range-limited, lossy, and delayed acoustic links with 6-DOF AUV dynamics. On a multi-target visit benchmark with 4 AUVs and 12 static tasks, the proposed method maintains at least 99.8 % task completion across all

title: Enhancing Cross-Border Financial Transactions with AI-Driven Blockchain Solutions - A Deep Neural Network Approach
authors: Manjula Pattnaik, Kathiresan Jayabalan, Rambabu Nalagandla, Deiveegan Arumugam, I Mohana Krishna, N.Padmavathi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Combining blockchain technology with artificial intelligence (AI) and more especially Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) produces a possibly revolutionary outcome meant to solve inefficiencies in global financial transactions. Conventional systems typically have drawbacks about foreign payments including costly fees, long processing times, and fraud risk. Solutions that boost the speed, openness, and security of transactions will help to fit the growing globalization of financial systems. This paper investigates how artificial intelligence systems driven by DNNs might be combined with blockchain systems to change world financial transactions. Blockchain technology guarantees that transaction records are transparent, unchangeable, and safe even while DNN models improve fraud detection, risk assessment, and dynamic optimization of transaction paths. The proposed system uses smart contracts to automatically follow global financial regulations. It also real-time optimizes money exchange rates using DNN-based predictive analytics. Our method consists in deep neural network algorithms trained on massive databases of transaction records and blockchain ledger systems. Among the main challenges the answer tackles are fraud prevention, scalability, and latency. Artificial intelligence improves operational efficiency as well as decision-making capacity; distributed ledger technology (DLT) promises decentralized and tamper-proof records. The results show that processing times and transaction costs have dropped, our capacity to prevent fraud has developed as well as our security has improved. Taken together, artificial intelligence and blockchain technology generate a scalable and effective architecture fit for use in many financial ecosystems.

title: Data Stream Processing Effectiveness in Heterogeneous Computing Environments
authors: Khaled Arsalane, Guillaume Pierre
publish: Proceedings of the 41st ACM/SIGAPP Symposium on Applied Computing
abstract: None

title: Structure-Aware Diffusion Policy for Bimanual Cooperative Manipulation_supp1-3701562.mp4
authors: Shiqi Li
abstract: Bimanual cooperative manipulation involves critical closed-chain contact phases in which task success depends on maintaining inter-arm geometric consistency while mitigating antagonistic interaction. We present a structure-aware diffusion policy that conditions action generation on an explicit cooperative representation, rather than inferring coordination implicitly from raw multimodal inputs. The representation combines (i) Cooperative Dual Task-Space (CDTS) descriptors computed from forward kinematics, which decompose bimanual geometry into absolute motion and relative configuration, and (ii) a gravity-compensated force-difference cue computed from left–right end-effector forces to capture interaction inconsistency. To integrate perception with these structured priors, we design a hierarchical fusion module in which bidirectional vision–force cross-attention first aligns cross-modal evidence, followed by CDTS-guided cross-attention that uses structural tokens as queries to extract coordination-relevant features. Real-robot experiments on dual-arm cooperative carrying across objects of varying sizes and appearances demonstrate improved closed-chain stability and more robust performance across the evaluated object variations, increasing the overall success rate from 58% to 89% compared with a vision-dominant diffusion baseline.

title: Co-deterioration Study of Nanoparticle-Modified Oil-Paper Insulation Systems Under Electric and Thermal Fields Based on Molecular Dynamics
authors: Haoxi Cong, Linghe Kong, Xuan Zhang, Junzhang Li, Xuefeng Hu, Qingmin Li, Yangchun Cheng, Li Xie, Hua Zheng, Xuelei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Natural ester insulating oils possess advantages such as high flash point and biodegradability. However, their low oxidative stability and high kinematic viscosity limit their application in transformers. During actual operation, transformers are subjected to the coupled effects of electric and thermal fields, which accelerate the degradation of the oil-paper insulation system. In this study, molecular dynamics simulations are employed to construct oil-paper insulation models modified with three types of nanoparticles, namely Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>, Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>, and TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. Systematic analyses are conducted on the elastic modulus and mean square displacement of insulating paper under various electric and thermal field conditions, as well as the fractional free volume and cohesive energy density of oil-paper insulation systems, and the shear viscosity of natural esters. The results indicate that all three types of nanoparticles can enhance intermolecular interactions and suppress deterioration processes under coupled electric and thermal fields. Among them, the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>-modified model exhibits the best overall performance. This study elucidates the microscopic mechanisms underlying nanoparticle-modified oil-paper insulation systems and provides theoretical support for improving the safe operation of transformer insulation systems.
