abstract: Present work proposes a predictive localization outcome when compared to Swarm Artificial Neural Network (SANN), Spatio-Temporal Neural Genetic Algorithm (STNGA) and Linear Ridge Radial Basis Function (LRRBF). To locate node is a pivotal process for providing increased reliability, energy efficiency and performance of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs). The traditional approach such as the Distance Vector–Hop (DV–HOP) algorithm are error prone and they have very large localization errors especially in both large-scale networks randomly deployed. It is observed that the HCMSPSO–DV–HOP model enhances hop size estimation using increased level of swarm-based optimization, but there is a scope for further improving its precision and stability among different deployment conditions. A novel Hybrid learning scheme of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Genetic Algorithms (GAs) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) based on loss-function corrected HCMSPSO-DV-HOP is introduced to improve the localization accuracy in existing framework. During training, PSO drives the solution refinement additionally helping faster and more stable convergence to global optimum as well. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the ANN–GA–PSO fusion model presents a more predictive localization outcome in comparison to SANN, STNGA and LRRBF. Other performance metrics (MSE, RMSE and MAE) show that the new approach not only reduces prediction error but also eases training.

title: Explainable Deep Learning Pipeline for Multimodal Biomedical Signal Interpretation
authors: Dhannya J, Pallavi B. Narkhede, Chennupalle Venkatasai Raghavendra Reddy, Parnapalli Maruthi, K.Suresh Babu, Aswathy N Rajan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: This research presents an explainable deep learning signal interpretation pipeline of multi-modal data of biomedical signals comprising of more than one mode (EEG, ECG and physiological sensors). For this, we pose a Transformer based multifunction fusion architecture and cross-modal attention utilities which is helpful in resolving temporal dependencies and signal-to-signal correlations. The pipeline utilises both GradCAM++ and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), for interpretability of the model, and will provide transparent insights in the decision making process of the model to clinicians. Our framework is build on PyTorch Lightning to ease and efficient model development and uncertainty quantification will be Monte Carlo dropout. The architecture has 94.3% accuracy in performing the task of classification of diseases and at the same time retaining its computational efficiency. Explainablity modules that are used to generate the visualization of attention maps showing the critical sections of the signals as well as feature importance values. Extensive validation on MIMIC-IV and PhysioNet database prove the superiority of this autonomy on compared to the conventional machine learning approaches establishing such framework to be a robust tool of automated biomedical signal analysis in precision medicine applications.

title: Decoding Automation Narratives in Heritage Education and Tourism: A Google Trends Inquiry into Optimization Dynamics
authors: Mohammad Irfan, Kashish Ajit Singh, Saikat Gochhait, Zafrul Allam
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustaining Heritage: The Future of Conservation: Embracing Technology and Innovation for Sustainable Heritage (ICSH)
abstract: An evaluation of integrating automation into heritage education and tourism with the help of Workplace Automation Systems (WAS) effects on Optimization with the help of Cointegration Causality Analysis to measure these effects. The research uses Google Trends data (2020-2025) to understand the Workplace Efficiency (WPE) and Employee Wellbeing (EWB) and Decision Quality (DQ) and Operational Safety (OS) relations through Graphical Trend Analysis and Augmented Dickey Fuller (ADF) Test and Johansen’s Cointegration Test and Granger Causality Test and Multiple Linear Regression Analysis. Automation systems lead to distinct efficiency improvements in workplaces that preserve both employee health and superior decision output. The Granger Causality Test proves that important workplace variables form a reciprocal relationship because automation systems advance performance enhancement. Cointegration analysis shows that automated systems create lasting connections with efficient processes which would benefit performance. Standards regarding workforce transition, ethical considerations and cybersecurity threats still need to be addressed in order to achieve successful implementation. Strategic Artificial Intelligence (AI) implementation together with trained workforce programs along with regulatory guides must be established to produce optimal automation advantages through risk management techniques. The research delivers operational directions to the heritage education and tourism through Human Centered Automation (HCA) for their journey toward lasting workplace transformation. A strategic HCA framework is suggested in the paper to facilitate sustainable and culturally responsive automation for architectural heritage.

title: Dynamic Topology Control Strategies for Enhanced Connectivity in Mobile Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Ramy Riad Al-Fatlawy, Saloni Bansal, Mudit Mittal, Arun Pratap Srivastava, Akhilesh Kumar Khan, Navdeep Dhaliwal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: The burgeoning domain of Mobile Wireless Sensor Networks (MWSNs) is under constant evolution, driven by the need for robust connectivity and coverage in a plethora of applications, from environmental monitoring to critical infrastructure surveillance. Dynamic topology control strategies stand at the forefront of this transformation, ensuring that the inherently mobile sensor nodes maintain effective communication despite their erratic movement patterns. This paper presents a comprehensive review of the existing literature, followed by an in-depth analysis of innovative topology control strategies that promise enhanced connectivity, optimized energy consumption, and improved overall network performance. Through simulations and real-world case studies, we demonstrate the efficacy of these strategies, underscoring the vital role of adaptive, predictive, and efficient network reconfiguration in the dynamic landscape of MWSNs.

title: Mechanism and Suppression of Low-Frequency Oscillations in Lightly Loaded V/f-Controlled Induction Motors
authors: Min Wu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: Light-load V/f-controlled induction motors frequently experience low-frequency oscillations driven by the inverter dead-time effect, specifically through equivalent resistance and zero-current clamping. To mitigate this, a comprehensive pulse-width strategy integrating compensations for pulse width, device forward voltage drop, and zero-current clamping is proposed. Experiments confirm this method effectively reduces phase current harmonics and restores complete system stability.

title: An Improved Multi-Target Tracking Algorithm for Intelligent Transportation Perception Based on Adaptive Association and Trajectory Smoothing
authors: Ru-Ping Li, Fang-Sheng Wu, Dong-Dong Shi
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: To address the issues of trajectory jitter, weak data association robustness, and limited tracking accuracy in multi-target tracking for intelligent transportation perception systems, this paper proposes an improved algorithm named SORT++, which is built upon the standard SORT (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking) framework. The improvements include: (1) an observation-noise-aware adaptive association threshold to enhance data association accuracy under dynamic sensor noise; (2) a velocity moving average smoothing mechanism to suppress trajectory fluctuations caused by sensor noise and model mismatch; (3) a weighted correction strategy in the Kalman filter update stage to improve state estimation stability. Experimental results on simulated traffic scenarios show that compared with KF, EKF, and standard SORT, the proposed SORT++ reduces the average root mean square error (RMSE) by 40.8%–56.5% and the average speed standard deviation (SSD) by 52.0%–68.4%, while maintaining a real-time inference speed of 1.6 ms per frame, which is suitable for vehicle-mounted or roadside embedded perception devices.

title: The CRISP Framework: Enhancing AI Explainability Through Context-Aware Prompts for Clearer and More Interpretable Responses
authors: Yvonne Jegede, Dorcas Addo, Atef Mohamed Shalan
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: This study evaluates the effectiveness of the CRISP (Context, Role, Iteration, Specificity, and Precision) prompt engineering framework in enhancing the interpretability, accuracy, and user alignment of AIgenerated responses. Four key metrics: precision, specificity, relevance and clarity, were employed to compare responses generated from CRISP-based prompts against those from generic prompts. The results indicate a substantial improvement in relevance, with a 46.82% increase, highlighting CRISP’s strong ability to produce contextually grounded and semantically aligned outputs. Precision and specificity improved by 3.91%, reflecting the capacity of CRISP to strengthen in generating focused, context-rich outputs. However, clarity declined by 2.10%, suggesting that the increased detail and structure introduced by CRISP may result in denser, less easily digestible responses. These findings demonstrate the impact of CRISP on response quality and point to the need for future refinement that better balance depth with clarity. Limitations include the small prompt sample, limited domain diversity, and reliance on automated metrics (TFIDF and cosine similarity) rather than human evaluation, which may not fully capture perceived clarity. Future research should integrate human and automated assessments to better evaluate CRISP’s real-world performance

title: The Impact of Short Video Content on TikTok on Gen Z's Purchasing Behavior
authors: Tran Ha Vy, Nguyen Thi Nhu Quynh, Le Trinh Thanh Tam, Ngo Hai Quynh
publish: 2025 Conference on Digital Economy and Fintech Innovation (DEFI)
abstract: The explosion of short video platforms (e.g. TikTok) has greatly changed consumer behavior, especially among Generation Z, which in turn has greatly affected the way businesses market their products. This study aims to explore and measure the influence of several factors on Generation Z's purchasing behavior in the context of short video marketing by addressing three main research questions: (1) What factors and their influence on Generation Z's purchase intention? (2) What strategic solutions can enhance the positive impact of these factors? Quantitative research methods were conducted with a sample size of 300 people aged 15-30. AMOS 20 was employed to calculate the parameters and evaluate the fit of the proposed model. The results were as expected with the strong influence of short video content, entertainment and social interaction on user attitudes, which in turn lead to their actual purchasing behavior. Interestingly, user trust has no relationship with their attitudes. This highlights the uniqueness of digital marketing, where entertainment, emotional appeal, and short video content and community interaction are providing a more authentic experience, and may overshadow the role of user trust in shaping attitudes. The study boldly reinforces the trend of leveraging user-generated contributions and "Mood broad" short video strategies from marketers to enhance user purchase behavior and engagement. A unique result, integrating emotion, entertainment, and social into platform models, has contributed to expanding the TAM and TPB theories, thereby explaining the nature and decision-making process of Gen Z in the digital environment. Cross-platform and cross-cultural comparative studies to further examine the role of trust in this model are encouraged to conduct a more comprehensive view.

title: Transport Protocol Switching Mechanism for Throughput Optimization in Ad Hoc Networks
authors: Yizhen Pan, Xin Xu, Liangdong Wei, Jun Cai
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th World Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (WCCCT)
abstract: Ad Hoc networks face significant challenges for reliable data transport due to limited spectral resources and dynamic channel conditions. This paper investigates the throughput performance of four reliable transport protocols—TCP, SCTP, UDT, and Mockets—under varying packet loss conditions in wireless environments. Experimental results demonstrate that while TCP and SCTP perform well under low-loss conditions (<2%), the middleware-based Mockets protocol significantly outperforms the other three protocols in high-loss environments (>3%). Based on these findings, we propose a protocol switching mechanism using a 2% packet loss threshold. Experimental evaluations show that this mechanism achieves 34MB total data transfer within a 60-second window, outperforming standalone TCP (10MB) and Mockets (24MB), thereby enhancing overall network throughput.

title: Toxicological Impact of Heavy Metals During Sustainable Brick Production from Bagasse, Fly Ash, and Cement
authors: Pratik Krishna, Santosh Verma
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: This study looks at the effects of heavy metals during the production of sustainable bricks made from fly ash, bagasse, and cement. Fly ash and bagasse, which are industrial and agricultural by-products, have a lot of potential for eco-friendly construction because they are abundant and help reduce waste. However, trace heavy metals like Pb, Cd, Cr, As, and Zn must be carefully evaluated to ensure safety for the environment and human health. We conducted tests on the raw materials and the final brick samples. The results showed that heavy metal levels remained within safe limits because they were effectively trapped in the cement mix. The final bricks had acceptable compressive strength (3.65 N/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) and and did not show any harmful or toxic properties. This study concludes that bricks made from fly ash, bagasse, and cement are safe for the environment, non-toxic, and promote sustainable construction while lowering the carbon footprint and addressing waste disposal issues.

title: SYNE: A Biologically-Inspired Decision Neural Network via Stochastic Delay Differential Equations
authors: Langhua Ju, Xinling Zhang
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: Modeling the neural mechanisms of decision-making is a major challenge in developing brain-inspired intelligence. Existing computational models, such as drift-diffusion models (DDM) and reinforcement learning (RL), address only subsets of these aspects in isolation, lacking a unified dynamical framework. Here we propose SYNE (Synaptic Nexus Entropy Network), a biologically-inspired decision neural network based on stochastic delay differential equations. SYNE integrates four core biological principles—dynamic homeostasis, competitive selection, history dependence, and intrinsic noise—into a single system. Theoretical analysis proves the existence, uniqueness, and local exponential stability of the solution. Experiments on multi-armed bandits, mouse maze navigation, and multi-agent market games show that SYNE achieves or exceeds state-of-the-art methods in exploration efficiency, risk adaptation, and dynamic coordination. Sensitivity analyses reveal functional specialization of components and provide parameter guidance.

title: Optimization and Simulation of a High-Speed Data and Power Transmission System Based on Partial Coil Coupling and DQPSK
authors: Dezhi Zhang, Liyan Zhang
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: To enable reliable real-time communication between the isolated primary and secondary sides in an inductive wireless power transfer (IWPT) system, this paper proposes a novel synchronous wireless power and data transmission (SWPDT) system utilizing quadrature differential phase shift keying (4DPSK) modulation. To address the severe high-impedance bottleneck faced by high- frequency data signals when passing through the primary power coil, the proposed system introduces a unique partial coil coupling topology. This structure provides a dedicated low-impedance bypass path for the 4 MHz data carrier, significantly improving signal injection and extraction efficiency. On the secondary side, multi- stage LC series resonators and high-pass filter networks are used to separate the data signal from the 85 kHz power signal, followed by accurate data recovery via a coherent I/Q dual-path differential demodulation chain. Simulation results validate the superiority of the proposed system. At kilowatt-level power (output power of 1026 W), the system achieves a high power transmission efficiency of 94.56%.Notably, the data transmission rate reaches 1 Mbps with a bit error rate of 0, demonstrating that the proposed topology significantly enhances system data throughput while effectively suppressing high-power cross-interference.

title: A Practical Methodology for Implementing and Tuning Analog PID-Based PWM Controllers
authors: Joey Florent, Renee Anderson, Wala Saadeh
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: This paper presents a practical, reusable workflow for implementing and tuning analog proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controllers that drive a PWM power stage. The methodology emphasizes continuous-time signal paths and hardware-tunable responses, enabling stable closed-loop control without sampling latency. A constant-current regulation prototype validates the approach using a shunt-sense instrumentation amplifier and an op-amp PID network whose output modulates a comparator-based PWM. With an RC feedback time constant of 5 ms and a switching frequency of 5 kHz, the system achieves steady-state current error as low as 0.2% and critically damped settling of 8 ms under resistive load steps. Tuning guidelines are provided for underdamped, overdamped, and critically damped regimes, together with practical insights on PWM frequency selection, RC filtering, gate-drive considerations, and PCB layout for noise mitigation. Although demonstrated for current regulation, the workflow is directly applicable to other analog PWM-controlled plants such as motor drives, LED drivers, and power regulation loops.

title: The Autonomous Battlefield: AI Arms Race in Cybersecurity, Offensive Tactics, and the Need for a Global Framework
authors: Rajdip Chatterjee, Shameemul Haque
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: The use of artificial intelligence is rapidly growing in cybersecurity as both a strong attack weapon and a necessary defensive weapon. In the first part of the article, Offensive Tactics, we discuss how attackers leverage AI technology as part of their social engineering attacks (e.g. through sophisticated types of phishing based on multiple principles including respecting authority and generating authority). The second part of the article, Intelligent Countermeasures, describes how security professionals utilize AI-based threat hunting to examine large data sets for signs of hidden and persistent threats through the detection of subtle differences and changes in systems. The final section of the article, The Future of Digital Conflict, describes how these two fighting systems will increasingly compete in independent, machine-tomachine warfare without intervention by humans. Finally, the conclusion states that technology on its own cannot create a secure environment. Rather, we need fundamentally different structures that take into account international policies, ethical standards, and use of AI for surveillance in order to manage this global digital conflict.

title: Lung Nodule Detection and Segmentation using Linear Karnal SVM
authors: Maruf-Uz-Zaman, Prabhat Kumar Ravi, Nitin Chakole, Rajeev Ranjan, T. V. Hyma Lakshmi, Sonu Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Lung cancer is mostly popular cancer in the world. Different technique used to find accuracy, speed and sensitivity. Computer Aided Detection (CAD) system is easy and common technique to detect lung cancer. In this work discuss about normal nodule, small nodule and micro nodule lung image and their segmentation. Feature extraction, pre-processing, data analysis and classifier are common step to detect lung nodules including linear-kernel function and different support vector machine. Accuracy compares with kernel function accuracy and discus about kernel function algorithms. Proposed work normal lung nodules accuracy 85.7% and after kernel function accuracy improved to 88.5% and small lung nodules accuracy 84.7% and after filter 89.5% and micro lung nodules accuracy is 89.7% and improved accuracy rate is 91.6% and also discuss about their comparative analysis.

title: Monotone Circuit Complexity of Matching
authors: Bruno Cavalar, Mika Göös, Artur Riazanov, Anastasia Sofronova, Dmitry Sokolov
publish: Proceedings of the 58th Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing
abstract: None

title: Near Optimal Hardness of Approximating 𝑘-CSP
authors: Dor Minzer, Kai Zhe Zheng
publish: Proceedings of the 58th Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing
abstract: None

title: Approximating Gains-from-Trade in Matching Markets
authors: Moshe Babaioff, Aviad Rubinstein, Xizhi Tan, Kangning Wang

title: Multi-Modal Fine-grained Discriminative Cues Transfer for Efficient Person Re-Identification
authors: Shenyu Xu, Boqian Liu, Hong Zhang, Haojie Li, Yi Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: In Person Re-identification(ReID), multi-modal features such as text can complement image features with discriminative semantic cues. Existing methods use CLIP to optimize learnable textual prompts, which are then utilized to enhance the features for ReID. However, these textual prompts are often susceptible to background noise and struggle to capture fine-grained discriminative attributes. Moreover, during the inference phase, networks of the multi-modal, such as the text encoder, must remain operational alongside the image encoder, thereby increasing computational overhead. In this paper, we design a Multi-Modal Fine-grained Discriminative Cues Transfer model for efficient person ReID. To address the limitation of existing text modal in capturing fine-grained discriminative information, we propose a Multi-Modal Fine-grained Knowledge Extraction (MMFE) module. This module leverages pre-trained foundation models to generate detailed person attribute descriptions and anatomical structure characterizations, and encodes both modals into multi-modal representations for enhancing the visual features. For improved inference efficiency, we design an Explicit-Implicit combined Knowledge Injection (EIKI) module. Through explicit feature alignment and implicit multi-modal attention mechanism, EIKI guides the image encoder to focus on fine-grained discriminative semantics and structural information within images. This design enables the method to effectively utilize multi-modal knowledge while discarding auxiliary modalities-related networks during inference. Experimental results on multiple public datasets demonstrate that our approach not only enhances ReID accuracy but also maintains computational efficiency with reduced resource consumption, making it particularly suitable for edge devices.

title: Optical Multi-STAR-IRS Assisted Underwater Wireless Communication System
authors: Rehana Salam, Vivek Ashok Bohara, Anand Srivastava
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Optical intelligent reflecting surfaces (OIRSs) have emerged as a promising solution for enhancing reliability, coverage, and energy efficiency (EE) in optical wireless communication networks. However, their application in underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) remains limited due to severe attenuation and the lack of adaptive non-line-of-sight (NLOS) support. To address these challenges, this paper proposes two novel optical simultaneously transmitting and reflecting intelligent reflecting surface (OSTAR-IRS) architectures: (i) a hybrid mirror-array and liquid crystal-based design (H-OSTAR-IRS), and (ii) a standalone liquid crystal-based OSTAR-IRS (LC-OSTAR-IRS). The proposed system integrates non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) under energy splitting (ES) and mode switching (MS) operations with optimized power allocation. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed H-OSTAR-IRS architecture achieves higher aggregate spectral efficiency (ASE) compared to LC-OSTAR-IRS, with improvements of up to approximately 20-30% depending on the multiple access scheme. In addition, RSMA consistently improves fairness across users. Furthermore, the MS mode achieves execution times below 3 seconds, highlighting its practical suitability and low computational complexity.

title: Diffusion-based Data Augmentation and Knowledge Distillation with Generated Soft Labels for Oil Spill Segmentation Learning on Satellite SAR Images
authors: Jaeho Moon, Jeonghwan Yun, Jaehyun Kim, Jaehyup Lee, Munchurl Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Semantic segmentation often suffers from a compounded scarcity of data, where both input observations and ground-truth labels are limited. This problem is particularly severe in remote sensing tasks such as oil spill segmentation using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where rare event occurrence and the difficulty of SAR image acquisition result in limited training data. To address this limitation, we propose a diffusion-based Data Augmentation with Knowledge Transfer (DAKTer) strategy. Our DAKTer strategy enables a diffusion model to generate SAR oil spill images along with soft label pairs, which offer richer class probability distributions than segmentation masks (i.e. hard labels). Also, for reliable joint generation of high-quality SAR images and soft labels with high correspondence, we introduce an SNR-based balancing factor aligning the noise corruption process of both modalities in diffusion models. By leveraging the generated SAR images and soft labels, a student segmentation model can learn robust feature representations without teacher models trained for the same segmentation task, improving its ability to segment oil spill regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DAKTer strategy effectively transfers the knowledge of per-pixel class probabilities to the student segmentation model to distinguish the oil spill regions from other look-alike regions in the SAR images. Our DAKTer strategy boosts various segmentation models to achieve superior performance with large margins compared to other generative data augmentation methods.

title: Stability Analysis of Control Systems with First-order Reset Elements, a Frequency-Domain Approach
authors: Ali Hosseini, Hassan HosseinNia
abstract: Reset control systems (RCSs) can achieve performance beyond that of conventional linear time-invariant (LTI) controllers, while also allowing analysis directly in the frequency domain using measured frequency response functions (FRFs). Despite this potential, existing frequency-domain stability approaches are typically restricted to specific RCS architectures and commonly depend on parametric plant models, which limits their applicability in practice. In this paper, a generalized Hβ framework is developed for the most comprehensive class of RCS structures, incorporating pre-, post-, and parallel LTI filters, as well as nonzero after-reset values. Based on this formulation, an FRF-based representation corresponding to the Hβ transfer function is derived, and frequency-domain sufficient conditions are established to certify the Hβ-based quadratic stability criterion. As a result, the proposed framework enables direct FRF-based assessment of quadratic stability and convergence for the considered class of reset control systems, using the measured plant FRF together with the known controller and filter transfer functions, without requiring an explicit parametric plant model. The effectiveness and practical relevance of the method are demonstrated through an illustrative industrial case study.

title: Direct Multi-Source AoA Tracking for Passive UAV Surveillance Under Heterogeneous Signal Conditions
authors: Jingchao You, Zhikun Chen, Fuzhang Wang, Zhibin Chen, Hao Ye
abstract: Passive tracking of multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) using distributed 5G infrastructure represents a promising alternative approach in GNSS-limited or denied environments. However, in complex urban scenarios, this task remains highly challenging, as passively intercepted UAV signals often exhibit severe signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) imbalances, while occlusions lead to time-varying target cardinality. Moreover, due to the non-cooperative nature of multiple UAVs, measurements at each receiver are inherently aggregated rather than target-resolved. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a direct angle-of-arrival (AoA) tracking framework based on the δ-generalized labeled multi-Bernoulli (δ-GLMB) filter, which operates directly on received array measurements without explicit AoA estimation. A feature-space spectral reconstruction mechanism is first developed to suppress strong-signal dominance at the covariance level, thereby restoring the detectability of weak UAV signals under large SNR disparities. Building upon the reconstructed AoA feature representation, a novel measurement separation method (NMSM) is introduced, enabling reliable construction of association cost matrices despite inherently aggregated array observations. Furthermore, a multi-station fusion scheme with weighted likelihoods is introduced to exploit spatial diversity for three-dimensional position tracking. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust and accurate multi-UAV tracking under SNR disparities exceeding 25 dB, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art RFS-based approaches in terms of tracking accuracy, cardinality consistency, and convergence probability.

title: MmWave NLOS Sensing under Rough Relay Surface: Challenges and Solutions
authors: Guanghua Liu, Xiaotong Lu, You Xu, Haoran Yuan
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) perception is essential for preventing potential collisions and finds applications in areas such as autonomous driving, target localization, and human recognition in urban environments. Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar distinguishes itself by robust NLOS sensing abilities. However, in urban environments, the reflective properties of relay surfaces have become complex. Diverse construction materials, intricate architectural designs, and dynamic environmental variations introduce complexity, violating the conventional assumption of smooth, deterministic geometries and degrading NLOS perception performance. To improve mmWave NLOS sensing under these realistic conditions, we investigate the characteristics of rough relay surfaces and develop a sensing framework to manage multipath interference. Our approach specifically focuses on three specific challenges often overlooked in current research: precise detection under small-scale rough relay surfaces, high-resolution imaging under large-scale relay surfaces, and multipath scattering classification under uncertain surfaces. We establish scattering signal models under different roughness conditions and develop effective NLOS sensing systems based on the distribution characteristics of targets behind different rough surfaces, achieving accurate and reliable sensing.

title: Data-Driven Optimized Output Regulation for Markov Jump Linear Systems and Its Application
authors: Hao Shen, Zheng Huang, Jiacheng Wu, Jing Wang, Jinde Cao
abstract: The linear optimized output regulation problem (LOORP) for Markov jump linear systems (MJLSs) is investigated in this article. First, by improving previous methods for solving the regulator equations, a model-based scheme is proposed to address the LOORP of MJLSs. Subsequently, a reinforcement learning (RL)-based iteration scheme is developed, which enables the solution of the LOORP even when the system dynamics are partially unknown and the initial control gains are unstable. Finally, an LCL-coupled inverter-based distributed generation system is provided to demonstrate the performance of the proposed RL-based scheme.

title: Electronic Fratricide in Air Defence Operations While Countering Uas in Tactical Battle Areas
authors: M Y Khan, Pranjal Agarwal, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Electronic fratricide in air defence operations refers to the unintended interference, degradation or neutralisation of friendly sensors, communication networks, navigation aids and weapon control systems due to uncoordinated electronic emissions within a contested electromagnetic environment. The rapid proliferation of Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS), operating across diverse frequency bands with low radar crosssections, has compelled air defence forces to increasingly rely on electronic countermeasures, jammers, spoofers and directed energy weapons[1] [2]. While these measures are essential for countering hostile UAS, inadequate spectrum coordination and decentralised control can inadvertently disrupt friendly systems, resulting in loss of situational awareness, delayed engagements and increased risk of blue-on-blue incidents[2] [4]. This paper analyses the evolution of the UAS threat, its employment in contemporary conflicts, existing counter-UAS measures and the primary causes of electronic fratricide. Case studies from recent conflicts are examined to highlight operational risks, followed by recommendations to mitigate electronic fratricide through centralised command and control, integrated spectrum management, training and procedural reforms.

title: AI-Enhanced Learning Environments to Foster Communicative Competence in French. A Mixed-Methods Study with STEAM Students
authors: Miriam Iliana Navarrete Bear, Adriana Morales Rodriguez, Cristian Tatiana Segura Dominguez
abstract: This paper presents an educational innovation project designed to enhance communicative competence in French as a Foreign Language (FLE) through the integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools. Conducted between November 2024 and May 2025 at PrepaTec Campus Tampico, Tecnológico de Monterrey (Mexico), the study involved 68 students aged 1618, enrolled in the Multicultural High School Program. These learners, coming from Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics (STEAM) disciplines, participated in FLE courses at levels 2, 4, and 5, corresponding to CEFR A1-B1. The project integrated applications such as ChatGPT, TecGPT, Hello History, Fliki, HeyGen, Suno, Piclumen, Hailou, and SkillStudio to strengthen oral and written production in digitally enriched learning environments. A mixed-methods approach was applied: quantitative data were obtained through pre- and post-tests (TCF and DELF-style simulations), while qualitative insights emerged from student feedback, audio/video productions, and teacher observations. Results indicate significant gains in fluency, pronunciation, argumentation, accuracy, and learner autonomy, with increased motivation and confidence. This study demonstrates that AI-enhanced environments can foster communicative competence in non-immersive contexts, offering replicable models for integrating emerging technologies into language education.

title: Message from the Publication Chair 2026 IEEE World Engineering Education Conference (EDUNINE)
authors: N/A
abstract: On behalf of the publication committee, I am delighted to welcome you to our Edunine 2026, the tenth edition in Mexico. A long history of knowledge generation in the Latin American context; also, anecdotes, memories, and, principally, we are part of a strong Educational Community. Edunine was established to contribute to our understanding of the impact of education on society and to develop methodologies and strategies in engineering education.

title: Photoacoustic signal from hemoglobin target
authors: Isabel Nunes Ramalho da Rocha, Juliana Oliveira da Silva Fernandes, Raymundo Cordero Garcia, Krishynan Santhy Fernandes Meirelles Araujo, Beatriz Ferreira de Carvalho Patricio, Ana Beatriz Furlanetto Pacheco, João Carlos Machado
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Photoacoustic (PA) systems have been extensively studied for their potential to obtain functional information from biological organs and tissues. Studies report the potential of endogenous molecules, such as hemoglobin (Hb), to generate photoacoustic signals. This study presents an experimental evaluation of a PA system developed at the Ultrasound Laboratory of the Biomedical Engineering Program at COPPE/UFRJ. For this purpose, a rectangular phantom was constructed from a 2% agarose solution using a mold produced by 3D printing, which had internal channels molded by catheters, which were removed after the agarose solidified. Initially, graphite was analyzed, used as a positive control for optical absorption, followed by a solution of human Hb at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. Photoacoustic excitation was performed using a DPSS laser source operating at 1064 nm, and the acoustic signals generated were detected by an ultrasonic transducer. The results demonstrated the acquisition of clearly detectable photoacoustic signals for both absorbents, highlighting the sensitivity of the system to Hb detection, as found in the literature. This study validates the proposed experimental methodology and establishes a basis for future studies.

title: The Development of Foundations of Open Practices Online Courses
authors: Diana Andone, Vlad Mihaescu, Radu Vasiu, Silviu Vert, Andrei Ternauciuc, Marius Tataru
abstract: This paper introduces the analyses of the design, create, and pilot four modular courses - Licensing and Open Licensing, Open Education, Open Access and Open Science, and Open Innovation, as integrated in a micro-specialisation of Foundation of Open Practices - to equip students and staff across the E<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>UDRES<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> European Universities Alliance with the skills, knowledge, and credentials needed to engage with open practices. The course development methodology involved a collaborative, cross-institutional design process based on the EU DigComp 2.2 Digital Competences framework, Design-Based Research (DBR), intersected with the ABCtoVLE method and refined based on instructional design principles and the Open Educational Practices (OEP) framework. The integration of the Foundations in Open Practices training programme into the E<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>UDRES<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> Arena Virtual Learning Environment (VLE), built on Moodle, represents a strategic step toward creating a coherent, scalable, and pedagogically aligned digital learning ecosystem for these courses. The courses contribute to embedding openness within higher education curricula and align with European digital competence frameworks.

title: Optimizing Municipal Teams: Personalized Learning for T-Shaped Profile Development
authors: Klára Kubíčkova, Janka Marschalková, Zuzana Hruška, Leonard Walletzký
abstract: As Smart Cities mature from technological pilots to complex Cooperative Cities, the primary barrier to success is often not technical, but organizational. Traditional municipal structures rely on vertical silos that inhibit the crossdepartmental collaboration required for climate neutrality and system-level resilience. This paper argues that the Cooperative City requires a fundamental shift in human capital development toward T-shaped professionals-experts who combine deep vertical specialization with broad horizontal skills in boundary spanning and strategic collaboration. We present findings from a qualitative study of Czech municipalities identifying a critical gap in these horizontal competencies and a reactive approach to upskilling. In response, we introduce a Proof of Concept for an AI-driven system and explore the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to transform municipal education. We specifically identify the challenges of moving beyond static recommendations toward automated, individualized content generation. By synthesizing practical best practice examples into personalized learning paths, we demonstrate a vision for a “human infrastructure” that fosters true inter-institutional cooperation.

title: Interactive Graphical User Interface for Spectral Doppler Simulation and Experimentation
authors: Gilson M. Kanashiro, Joaquim M. Maia, Amauri A. Assef, Eduardo T. Costa
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: This paper presents an interactive computational environment developed for spectral Doppler simulation and experimentation, integrating B-mode image reconstruction and blood flow analysis. The system was implemented in MATLAB and provides customization features, including dynamic adjustment of insonation angle, spectral resolution, dynamic range, and baseline shift. Its modular architecture enables exploration of each stage of the Doppler pipeline - from raw data acquisition to spectral visualization - making it an effective tool for education and research in biomedical engineering. Preliminary results demonstrate that the environment enhances understanding of fundamental concepts of ultrasound signal processing and important principles such as aliasing, the relationship between pulse repetition frequency and resolution, and the impact of angle steering of plane waves on velocity estimation.

title: VPETD: A Generative AI Driven Novel Privacy Themed Cybersecurity Educational Game
authors: Abhishek Shah, Ankur Chattopadhyay, Levi Pfantz
abstract: This paper introduces a first of its kind generative artificial intelligence (AI) driven visual privacy themed educational game (VPETD), which is an enhanced version of the Visual Privacy Enhancing Technology (VPET) themed game. The VPETD game was designed to teach data privacy plus security concepts in an interactive and hands-on learning mode. Building upon the original VPET game, VPETD makes innovative use of generative AI in the form of Stable Diffusion, which is a text-to-image generator model, to create diverse character sets. VPETD introduces a novel Doppelganger feature, that enables players to select lookalike disguises using a slider-based interface. This Doppelganger functionality helps improve the game engagement and experience with a redesigned user interface and dynamically variant image sets. VPETD employs computer vision-based image processing techniques (via OpenCV) and multimedia synthesis to support de-identification driven image obfuscation tasks and to illustrate privacy through visual anonymity as an essence of how VPET works. To assess the potential value and efficacy of VPETD, we performed a user assessment study with a diverse set of participants, including K-12 learners plus college-level students, as well as educators, who played the game in our cybersecurity training camps and workshops. This paper shares and analyzes the preliminary data collected from these participants in the form of survey responses, as part of this conducted research study. Our study demonstrates the prospects of VPETD, increased participant interest plus awareness levels in privacy and security, plus a high recommendation rate for our game. In summary, our findings show that VPETD raises overall awareness plus understanding levels and drives meaningful engagement, thus highlighting its utility as a generative AI-powered educational game in an experiential learning setup.

title: First-Year Engineering Design: Active Learning with Rockets, Simulation, and AI
authors: Jorge Alvarez, Jorge Hidalgo, Juan-Gabino Diaz-Martinez, Irandi Gutiérrez-Carmona, Zahira Gabriela Cruz-Netro, L.F. Guevara-Chavez
abstract: This paper presents the design, implementation, and evaluation of the HydroRocket project, a project-based learning (PBL) experience for first-year engineering students. The activity combined the construction of a water-propelled rocket with digital simulation in MATLAB, data acquisition through Arduino-based sensors, and personalized tutoring supported by artificial intelligence. The primary objective was to enhance engineering design self-efficacy, defined as students' confidence in their ability to address complex design tasks. The validated Engineering Design Self-Efficacy Scale was administered to 62 students, yielding mean scores ranging from 3.56 to 4.05 with high internal consistency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\alpha=0.91)$</tex>. Quantitative results were reinforced by qualitative evidence. Additionally, prototype performance improved by 44% over successive iterations, providing objective evidence of design improvement. Findings indicate that the integration of emerging technologies into hands-on projects can foster motivation, autonomous learning, and the development of professional identity in engineering from the earliest stages of education.

title: A Learning Framework for Control Systems in Manufacturing Processes Using the FrED Platform
authors: David Ángeles-Rojas, Pedro Ponce, Bradley Russel, Brian Anthony
abstract: This paper presents a training framework using the Fiber Extrusion Device (FrED) from MIT to enhance manufacturing process education and strengthen the connection between control theory and practical implementation. A common challenge in engineering education is that students often struggle to relate theoretical concepts to real-world systems. This work addresses that gap through an integrated experimental approach. The framework links academic learning with industrial practice by guiding participants through data acquisition, system identification, and PID controller design using FrED's actuator. Experimental data are modeled with the least squares method to obtain a second order transfer function, allowing learners to observe how parameter estimation and gain tuning influence system behavior. Using MATLAB and Python for analysis and implementation, the methodology improves both conceptual understanding and practical competence, contributing to workforce development in smart manufacturing.

title: Reconstruction of Scan Scintigraphy and SPECT Images Using an Enhanced Iterative Blind Deconvolution Algorithm
authors: Letícia Alves Ferreira, Sérgio Querino Brunette, Bárbara Juarez Amorim, Eduardo Tavares Costa
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Image restoration is the process of estimating the original image content from a degraded image. It is assumed the main type of scintigraphy (scan) and SPECT images degradation is due to the Compton effect. In this paper we present a methodology for mitigating this effect by image deconvolution using the system PSF and dependent on the type of radiotracer and the patient phenotype. The methodology applies a blind deconvolution using Richardson-Lucy iterative algorithm along with other techniques, like automatic iterations interruption. Our approach consists of estimating the total PSF of the patient-detector system applying the blind deconvolution twice, using the PSF found on the first deconvolution, which has as parameters the lower energy window (Compton effect) phantom image and the image of the point source filled with the radiotracer of interest (<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">99m</sup>Tc MDP, <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">177</sup>Lut and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">131</sup>I). The methodology was applied on scintigraphy and SPECT images of the phantom Quality Nema-NU2-2007 filled with water and one of the radiotracers. Reconstructed images of the phantom presented a reduced Compton effect and a better edge definition, after analyzing the ECBM metric (related to the borders definition) - any gain greater than zero represents a better border definition. This metric presented a gain of over 100% in most of the images.

title: Signal-Compensated PI Control Based on Concurrent-Learning-Driven Optimal Tuning Parameters for Complex Ore Feeding Processes
authors: Ce Guo, Yajun Zhang, Rui Zheng, Yao Jia, Ning Liu, Tianyou Chai
abstract: Due to the influence of fluctuations in the particle size and throughput of ore from the upstream crushing stage, variations in the return flow of unqualified particles during grinding, and the frequent changes of unknown nonlinearities in the complex ore feeding process, it is difficult to apply existing disturbance-rejection control methods to control the feed rate within the target range. In this article, by utilizing the characteristic that the feed rate fluctuates around the target interval, a dynamic model of the feeding process is established. Based on the obtainable previous-time-step value of the unknown nonlinear dynamic system and the closed-loop tracking error generated by the unknown rate of change of this nonlinear system, and the PI closed-loop equation, a data-driven signal-compensator is designed by combining feedforward control with a one-step optimal control law. A signal-compensated PI controller is proposed. The performance index that minimizes the tracking error at each operating instant is introduced, and a concurrent-learning-driven optimal tuning method for parameters of both the PI controller and the compensator is proposed. Simulation and industrial application experiments demonstrate that the proposed control method is capable of controlling the tracking error within the target interval during the entire operational period.

title: Geometry-Prior Guided Coarse-to-Fine Stabilization of Full-Disk Solar EUV Image Sequences
authors: Qiyan Luo, Zhongli Fan, Mi Wang, Lekang Wen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Full- disk solar extreme ultraviolet (EUV) image sequences are fundamental for continuous monitoring of coronal dynamics in space-weather applications, but on-orbit platform and instrument disturbances introduce inter-frame jitter that disrupts temporal continuity. Solar EUV sequences further challenge generic stabilization pipelines due to strong nonrigid internal evolution, large low-texture regions, and background artifacts that induce unreliable correspondences. This article addresses full- disk sequence stabilization under a 2D similarity model and proposes a three-stage framework for FY-3E X-EUV sequences. First, we construct an edge-aware geometric representation using Sobel- gradient magnitude and a coarse-to-fine ring search to emphasize stable disk-edge structures. Second, we perform a local geometry-aware coarse correction by estimating a disk-center prior and inner- disk mask with a Gaussian-weighted disk-edge ring template, followed by a sub-pixel translation refinement via patch correlation. Third, we apply a global feature-aware fine correction by robustly estimating a similarity transform with an IGG-III weighted iterative solver. We introduce a FY-3E X-EUV full- disk stabilization benchmark of nine annotated sequences, including quiet and C/M-class flare cases, together with a reproducible evaluation protocol under translation, rotation, and larger frame-interval perturbations. We further report raw-sequence image-domain evaluation, quiet- versus-flare disaggregated analysis, and scale-drift sensitivity analysis. Extensive experiments on the benchmark demonstrate that the proposed method achieves consistently high accuracy and robustness and outperforms existing approaches.

title: LAWNs Meet SWIPT: Beamforming and Power Splitting Optimization for Predictive Control
authors: Jun Wu, Wenchao Liu, Weijie Yuan, Nanchi Su
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enabling sustainable connectivity in battery-limited low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs). This paper investigates a SWIPT-enabled LAWN system in which a multi-antenna base station (BS) simultaneously delivers control information and wireless energy to a fleet of uncrewed aircraft systems (UASs) via power splitting. In particular, the BS remotely guides the UASs to accurately track predefined reference trajectories toward their destinations while avoiding multiple mobile no-fly zones (NFZs). To guarantee collision-free path planning, we first construct smooth and safe reference trajectories using stream function theory. Then, a real-time optimization problem is formulated, which jointly takes into account the wireless control cost and energy sustainability by optimizing control inputs, transmit beamforming vectors, and the power splitting ratios. To address the resultant non-convex problem, a two-stage optimization framework is proposed. First, we develop a model predictive control (MPC)-based method to generate predictive control inputs. Subsequently, we derive a computationally efficient iterative algorithm to optimize the beamforming vectors and power splitting ratios by applying semidefinite relaxation (SDR) and successive convex approximation (SCA) techniques. We further prove that the SDR is tight for our formulation. Extensive numerical results demonstrate that our proposed design significantly outperforms benchmark schemes in terms of tracking accuracy and harvested energy, thereby validating its effectiveness for sustainable implementation in LAWN systems.

title: Enable Scalable and Secure ISAC-WPT in Wireless Scenarios
authors: Xiufeng Liu, Xiulong Liu, Xin Xie, Ke Wang, Jiuwu Zhang, Xinyu Tong, Zheng Gong, Keqiu Li
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) and Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) technologies enable the transmission of wireless waves to power battery-free user devices while simultaneously supporting their communication and environmental sensing functions. However, as the number of users increases in such scenarios, existing ISAC-WPT schemes face challenges of overload interference and security vulnerabilities. To this end, this paper proposes a Scalable and Secure ISAC-WPT (S²SCPT) scheme. Specifically, the uplink base station integrates OFDMA and NOMA technologies, utilizing a three-dimensional resource optimization strategy to suppress multi-user interference, which balances communication and sensing performance while supplying power to energy harvesting receivers (EHR). For security, a dynamic subcarrier hopping and directional power modulation scheme based on OFDMA is developed to enhance Physical-layer Key Generation (PKG); downlink users utilize cyclic codes and an ACK verification mechanism to locate subcarriers and protect WPT security. Experimental results indicate that, in multi-user scenarios, the proposed method increases the communication rate by up to 26% while maintaining sensing performance; during WPT, it reduces power consumption by 11% without compromising communication rates, and enhanced PKG demonstrates significant advantages in the system’s sum secret key rate.

title: Hybrid-CMLP: Hybrid CNN-MLP Networks for Low-to-standard-dose PET Synthesis
authors: Yuxin Xue, Mingyuan Meng, Lei Bi, Jinman Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Positron Emission Tomography (PET) is a critical modality in medical imaging for detecting abnormalities and diagnosing diseases. However, the radiation exposure from the radiotracer remains a significant concern. Synthesizing standard-dose PET (sPET) images from low-dose PET (lPET) offers a compelling solution, enabling high-quality imaging while substantially reducing radiation risks to patients. Existing PET synthesis methods predominantly rely on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which are inherently limited by their local receptive fields. Transformer-based architectures have been explored to address this limitation by modeling long-range dependencies through self-attention. However, the high computational and memory costs of self-attention restrict Transformers to downsampled feature maps, limiting their ability to model global anatomical and functional correlations at full resolution. Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), in contrast, offer a computationally efficient way to capture long-range dependencies at full resolution. Yet, existing MLP-based methods for PET synthesis lack mechanisms to capture localized features such as subtle tissue textures that are critical for PET functionality. In this study, we propose a hybrid CNN-MLP network (Hybrid-CMLP) architecture for PET synthesis. Central to this architecture is a novel Hybrid-Syn block, which integrates axis-wise MLP branches for capturing global dependencies along spatial dimensions, and dual CNN branches for extracting both fine-grained and broad contextual features. To further enhance fine-grained reasoning, we introduce an Adaptive Fusion Mechanism (AFM) that dynamically integrates global and local features based on spatial context. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that Hybrid-CMLP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

title: Mechanistic Enhancement of Electrical Performance in PEEK Insulation via Surface Gas-Phase Fluorination
authors: Hanbo Zheng, Pengcheng Zhang, Yi Li, Zhenlian An, Shenglin Zhou, Ziliang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Poly(ether ether ketone) exhibits significant potential for high-voltage insulation applications due to its excellent mechanical strength and thermal stability. However, the material's surface is prone to charge accumulation, which lowers the direct current flashover voltage and compromises its reliability in practical applications. This study systematically investigates the mechanism of performance enhancement in PEEK via surface fluorination, integrating molecular dynamics simulations with high-voltage DC flashover experiments. Simulation results indicate that the introduction of highly polar C–F bonds via fluorination strengthens intermolecular interactions, increases cohesive energy density by approximately 3.5%, enhances elastic modulus by over 15%, and significantly reduces the dielectric constant of PEEK. Experimental findings confirm that fluorination effectively suppresses surface tracking and flashover, with the DC flashover voltage of treated samples increasing by 23.03% relative to untreated ones. This improvement is primarily attributed to the fluorinated layer facilitating more efficient dissipation of surface charges, thereby enhancing resistance to electrical degradation and improving insulation reliability. The study demonstrates that moderate surface vapor-phase fluorination offers a viable strategy to enhance the overall insulation performance of PEEK without compromising its intrinsic properties, providing a robust theoretical basis and specific technical guidance for the rational design of next-generation polymer dielectrics.

title: Optimization Strategy for Micro Energy Grid Transactions in Multi-Level Energy Markets Based on Asymmetric Nash Bargaining
authors: Shan Liu, Kouyu Sun, Yiheng Liang, Kangyang Lv, Chenhui Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the advancement of the multi-level energy market, micro energy grid (MEG) operators are confronted with core issues such as insufficient transaction synergy and imbalance in the distribution of benefits among entities. This paper proposes a coordinated multi-level electricity market trading strategy and constructs an integrated multi-level energy trading framework covering the energy wholesale market, the peer-to-peer electricity market, and the shared energy storage leasing market. To balance efficiency and fairness in multi-level energy market coordination, an optimization method based on the asymmetric Nash bargaining theory is further developed. First, the strategy quantifies the actual contribution of each participating entity in multi-level transactions, and scientifically sets differentiated bargaining weights for all entities. On this basis, it realizes the global optimization of the overall operating cost of the MEG system while taking into account the benefit demands of each trading entity, thus achieving a reasonable and fair distribution of benefits. Finally, the alternating direction method of multipliers is adopted to solve the proposed optimization model, and case studies based on actual scenarios are conducted to verify the strategy. The results show that the proposed strategy can effectively improve the market coordination efficiency of the multi-level energy market, significantly reduce the comprehensive operating cost of the entire MEG system, and achieve a fair and reasonable distribution of trading benefits for each participating entity, which fully verifies the feasibility and effectiveness of the strategy.

title: Joint Frequency-Phase Estimation of Power Grid Signals Based on Frequency-Domain Attention Mechanism and Kalman Filter Synergy
authors: Junhao Feng, Tongyin Dai, Chen Liu, Shuguang Liu, Xiandong Zhang, Shuai Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Grid frequency is a core indicator of power quality. Its precise measurement serves as the benchmark for safe and stable grid operation while directly impacting the control accuracy of renewable energy grid integration. The increasing non-stationary characteristics of renewable power systems lead to frequent frequency fluctuations. Frequency measurement faces challenges such as slow dynamic response, cumulative phase errors, and low accuracy under scenarios involving sudden frequency changes, phase jumps, and harmonic pollution. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a joint frequency-phase estimation method for grid signals based on the synergy of frequency-domain attention mechanisms and Kalman filtering. This approach constructs a lightweight neural network architecture that extracts joint time-frequency domain features through cross-timescale convolutional layers. A frequency-domain attention module is designed to adaptively enhance response weights for fundamental and key harmonic components, effectively suppressing high-frequency noise and irrelevant frequency interference. Physical constraint phase encoding and Kalman dynamic correction mechanisms are introduced to eliminate phase jump errors and mitigate window truncation effects. Experimental validation demonstrates superior tracking accuracy and dynamic response performance under complex disturbance conditions.

title: Analysis and Prediction of Influencing Factors of Power Carbon Emissions in Guizhou Province Based on LMDI Decomposition and Multi-NAR Integrated Nonlinear Regression
authors: Chen Luo, Julong Chen, Xuepeng Mou, Bin Wang, Shiping Yang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Understanding the drivers of power carbon emissions is essential for improving emission forecasting and supporting low-carbon energy planning. Taking Guizhou Province as a case study, this study analyzes and predicts power sector carbon emissions during 2007–2022. The logarithmic mean Divisia index method is applied to decompose emission changes into multiple driving effects. The results indicate that urban per capita GDP, electricity intensity, and population scale are the dominant drivers. The GDP effect accounts for about 59% of the emission growth, while improvements in electricity intensity offset nearly 47% of the increase. Based on these factors, NAR, NARX, and FNN models are employed for forecasting analysis. Rolling test results show prediction errors ranging from 12.89% to 15.31% MAPE, providing a reliable evaluation of carbon emission trends in Guizhou.

title: System-Level Coordinated Control Model of Energy Storage Systems for Grid Security and Stability Analysis
authors: Yuhan Liu, Hanyang Dai, Xiong Xiao, Xiangtao Xiao, Xinli Song, Shaohang Hao, Yikang Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy, energy storage systems are playing an increasingly critical role in enhancing grid security and operational flexibility. This paper presents a system-level coordinated control model for energy storage systems participating in grid security and stability control, suitable for large-scale power system simulation. The study first analyzes the functions of energy storage systems in power flow control, frequency regulation, and voltage support. Based on this, a coordinated control framework is developed, integrating automatic generation control, automatic voltage control, security and stability control systems, and a priority-based weight allocation mechanism that coordinates command priorities with resource availability. The proposed model is validated through a four-machine two-area system simulation for secondary frequency regulation and a cascading fault case study based on an actual provincial power grid. Results demonstrate that the model accurately captures the multi-timescale dynamic characteristics of utility-scale energy storage systems and effectively supports grid security and stability analysis.

title: A Data-driven Approach to Synergistic Resilient Assessment and Investment Optimization for Sustainable Operation of Island Power Grids
authors: Yuetong Li, Haoyang Yu, Hongfan Li, Xueyong Ding, Shifeng Wang, Liansheng Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Island power grids face the core challenge of improving power supply reliability while controlling investment costs. To scientifically quantify the trade-off between these two factors and seek the optimal solution for social welfare, this paper constructs a data-driven resilience-economy synergistic assessment and investment decision optimization framework. Firstly, comprehensive system resilience metrics, total social costs, and marginal resilience costs are defined. Subsequently, a three-part methodology comprising power outage loss prediction, core metric calculation, and visualized decision-making is proposed. Finally, taking the Hainan power grid as a case study, five investment scenarios and three disaster severity levels are designed. The research demonstrates that there exists a clear optimal economic equilibrium point for grid resilience investment, with distinct patterns in marginal cost variations. Optimizing investment strategies can effectively enhance social welfare. This study provides a scientific quantitative analysis tool for refined grid planning and investment decision-making.

title: An Improved Graphical Method for Layout Optimization of Field-Limiting Ring Edge Termination
authors: Kiyoshi Takeuchi, Munetoshi Fukui, Takuya Saraya, Kazuo Itou, Toshihiko Takakura, Shinichi Suzuki, Hiroyuki Takase, Toshiro Hiramoto
abstract: An improved graphical method for optimizing the layout of field-limiting rings (FLRs) for edge termination is proposed. The required number of rings and the optimal combination of spacings between adjacent rings can be efficiently determined through a simple procedure based on TCAD simulations of three-terminal model structures. In a 1.2kV-class Si diode design example, the proposed method achieved 97% of the target breakdown voltage and 92% of the ideal parallel-plate breakdown voltage using 24 rings. Acomparison with a previously proposed method and numerical optimization results is also provided to further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Joint Secure Localization and Attack Detection for Vehicular Networks With Quantized Measurements: A Variational Bayesian Approach
authors: Yunfei Li, Zhengyao Zhang, Yiting Luo, Weiqiang Tan, Zheng Shi, Shaodan Ma
abstract: This letter addresses the critical challenge of secure and reliable localization in vehicular networks, where stringent bandwidth constraints and malicious attacks coexist. We propose the Quantized Variational Bayesian Learning-based Attack Detection and Secure Localization (QVBL-ADSL) algorithm, a novel method that jointly estimates the position of a target vehicle and identifies potential attacks using quantized measurements. By formulating the problem within a principled variational Bayesian framework, our approach efficiently approximates the complex posterior distribution, simultaneously handling the nonlinearities introduced by quantization and the distortions caused by adversarial interventions, while also accounting for node position uncertainty. The algorithm is computationally tractable and guaranteed to converge, making it suitable for real-time vehicular applications. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance in both localization accuracy and attack detection rate compared to state-of-the-art techniques, demonstrating particular efficacy in resource-limited and adversarial settings.

authors: N/A

title: Analysis and Optimization Framework for STAR-RIS-aided Short-Packet Systems with Covert Rate-Splitting Signaling
authors: Anh-Tu Le, Thai-Hoc Vu, Thien Huynh-The, Miroslav Voznak
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the performance of simultaneous transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS)-enabled short-packet communication (SPC) systems that employ covert rate-splitting (RS) to facilitate applications of Internet-of-Things (IoT). Under the generalized model of the α-η-κ-μ fading, we analyze covertness by first deriving a closed-form expression for the warden’s detection error probability (DEP) and then deducing the optimal detection threshold at which the DEP is minimized. From this optimal DEP, we guide the choice of the feasible power-allocation (PA) region at which the warden’s produced DEP is always beyond the minimal acceptable covertness. On the other hand, we develop mathematical frameworks for evaluating the system’s block-error rate (BLER) and the ergodic rate (ER), as well as providing guidelines on how to access their performance limits at high signal-to-noise ratio, especially the diversity orders and ergodic slopes of the users. Furthermore, we also propose to enhance the system performance by jointly optimizing the PA and RS coefficients in order to: (1) minimize the maximum BLER across private users subject to a minimum covertness requirement and (2) maximize the minimum ER across users subject to covertness and decoding constraints. Numerical results confirm the analytical expressions and show that the proposed optimization efficiently tunes the PA and RS coefficients to achieve the BLER and ER fairness objectives.

title: RLAPR: Reinforcement Learning-based Adaptive Fusion for Visual-LiDAR Place Recognition in Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Shuhuan Wen, Kaikang Bai, Peng Li, Qichao Zhao, Huaping Liu, F. Richard Yu
abstract: Place recognition is essential for robust long-term autonomy in autonomous vehicles. Single sensor is limited for complex environments, while multi-modal fusion of camera and LiDAR is effective. Static or attention-based fusion methods struggle to adapt to dynamic environments. To address the limitation, we propose a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework for dynamic weight adaptation in visual-LiDAR place recognition tailored for autonomous vehicle perception systems. In our approach, the RL agent acts as a meta-controller. It observes the current environmental state, which is derived from the characteristics of the input data, and learns a policy to dynamically adjust the contribution weights of the visual and LiDAR feature streams. This allows the network to dynamically favor the more reliable modality, such as LiDAR in low-light conditions or camera data in geometrically sparse scenes. We train and evaluate our model extensively on the challenging nuScenes dataset, which encompasses a diverse range of driving conditions across various times of day and weather scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our RL-based adaptive fusion strategy consistently outperforms existing static and attentional fusion methods, achieving superior recall rates and enhanced robustness to environmental variations for reliable vehicle navigation.

title: A Hierarchical Carbon Emission Factor Correction Method Considering Non-Grid-Connected Electricity
authors: Dawei Ma, Haotian Zhang, Ming Kong, Chao Ji, Wei Tang, Yang Su, Bin Xu, Chao Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate carbon emission factors (CEFs) are essential for tracking and reducing emissions in power systems. Traditional average CEF methods suffer from coarse granularity, while carbon flow tracing approaches are computationally intensive and unsuitable for large-scale grids. Moreover, both ignore the environmental benefits of non-grid-connected electricity (NGCE), such as local rooftop PV or self-consumed distributed generation. This paper proposes a hierarchical correction method for regional CEFs that accounts for NGCE. Based on electrical partitioning theory, the grid is divided into three layers: provincial, prefectural, and end-user. A recursive calculation strategy is adopted to compute CEFs for grid-connected electricity, while NGCE is attributed to local nodes for final correction. Case studies validate the method’s feasibility, showing improved spatiotemporal resolution and accurate reflection of distributed generation benefits. The approach offers a practical pathway for carbon accounting in regional power systems.

title: Mechanisms of Central Erosion on the Contact Interface With Convex Rail Structure
authors: Jinghan Xu, Minfu Liao, Xubin Li, Yifan Sun, Shengguo Xia, Lixue Chen, Chengxian Li
abstract: The armature and rail (A/R) interface in railguns experiences extreme conditions of high current and velocity, leading to severe melting erosion. Experimental and numerical studies have demonstrated that convex rails exhibit superior launch performance compared with flat ones, with recovered armatures revealing central erosion patterns distinct from those observed in flat rails. In this article, a coupled Multiphysics numerical model is developed, incorporating variations in contact pressure and area, armature acceleration, and temperature dependence of electrical conductivity. The electromagnetic–mechanical–thermal characteristics of the A/R interface at the beginning stage of the melting erosion are analyzed by investigating the distribution of interfacial heating sources. The results indicate that the melting erosion process is affected by two stages, including the initial stage and the acceleration stage. The interfacial Joule heat plays an important role in the temperature rise during the initial stage, whereas the frictional heat dominates the temperature distribution as the velocity increases. Eventually, central erosion patterns are generated on the A/R interface as the temperature reaches the melting point.

title: Control-Integrated Pipeline for Diseasome Analysis: A Case Study on Hypertension Comorbidities in U.S. Veterans_supp5-3698948.csv
