authors: N/A

title: A Methodology for Development and Characterization of PVA-Based Phantoms for Breast Elastography
authors: Alexey García Padilla, Ivonne Bazán Trujillo
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Breast elastography is a group of ultrasound imaging techniques based on tissue stiffness. Validation of these techniques requires constructing phantoms in a controlled manner to obtain reproducible acoustic and mechanical properties and ensure experimental repeatability. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used for phantoms due to their stability, durability, and ease of construction in a wide range of medical applications. This paper presents a methodology for fabricating PVA phantoms for breast elastography with two types of ultrasonic scatterers: graphite and Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>. A methodology was described for characterizing mechanically the phantoms using the Young's modulus by a computerized numerical control (CNC) system. Phantoms were constructed to emulate the Young's modulus of healthy breast tissue and benign tumors. The methodology employed for the construction of healthy tissue phantoms involved the use of a 10% PVA concentration, in combination with a single freeze-thaw cycle (FTC). The mean Young's modulus obtained using graphite was 6.12 kPa, while for Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> was 12.14 kPa for healthy tissue. For benign tissue phantoms, a 15% PVA concentration was utilized, in combination with two FTCs. The mean Young's modulus resulting from the use of graphite was found to be 38.50 kPa, while for Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, it was 39.21 kPa for benign tissue.

title: Beyond Geography: A Multidimensional Approach for New Geography Economic and Practical Application in Engineering Education
authors: Yuxuan Xiong
abstract: This paper builds on Krugman's New Economic Geography (NEG) by introducing a framework that extends traditional geography-only cluster analysis to include individual-level characteristics using clustering algorithms and Kernel Density Estimation (KDE). Rather than depending exclusively on geographic proximity, this multidimensional approach conceptually integrates diverse individual-level attributes to enable a richer and more detailed identification of agglomeration patterns. This paper offers guidance and a conceptual foundation for future empirical research aiming to capture the complex interaction between geographic and non-geographic factors that influence economic clusters. To better illustrate how the method generalizes to social sectors, we apply it conceptually to engineering education setting: institutions are embedded in a joint space of latitude-longitude and characteristics such as research intensity, faculty size, endowment, etc. We then relate multidimensional density to outcomes including graduate placement rates, research output (e.g., publications and citations), and external funding. Furthermore, to bridge macro-level patterns with micro-level behaviors, this paper proposes a visionary LLM-driven simulation, utilizing the latest agent-toagent (A2A) technology. In this framework, each individual in a cluster is represented by an LLM-based “agent” that is aware of its own profile and characteristics. These agents make decisions based on non-linear behaviors, leveraging the current state-of-the-art agent-based modeling (ABM) techniques. Although the agent-to-agent simulation is conceptual at this stage, it sets the foundation for future work that combines spatial econometric models with agent-based methods to gain a deeper understanding of how clusters form and evolve.

title: An Explainable MFCC-Based Deep Learning Framework for Song Emotion Recognition
authors: Hesamoddin Pourrostami, Mousa Nazari, Mohammad M. AlyanNezhadi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Automatically identifying the emotional elements within a piece of music and understanding music sentiment through audio captures an essential step within the realm of affective computing and music intelligence. This paper introduces a hybrid architecture that combines CNN, BiLSTM, and Attention, utilising CNN to analyse MFCC features extracted from audio signals, BiLSTM to model the audio sequence, and Attention to pinpoint emotionally salient regions. In addition to the mentioned features, the proposed architecture incorporates cost-sensitive learning to enhance performance and mitigate data imbalance. The MFCC-based deep learning framework is applied to song music in the RAVDESS dataset. The proposed method achieved an overall accuracy of 91.6% and a macro F1-score of 92.3%. Explainable AI, utilising Gradient-Based Feature Importance, focuses on the low-spectral to mid-spectral regions where the audio is emotionally and temporally impressive. These confirm that the proposed architecture is streamlined to label singing through music analysis emotionally. This can be extended to various applications, such as affective music in music therapy and Human-computer interaction.

title: Enhancing Cybersecurity Education with Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Virtual Teaching Assistant Approach
authors: Gaurab Baral, Sushant Shrestha, Mithlesh Sah, Ankur Chattopadhyay
abstract: This paper presents a novel research study based upon the development and usage of a unique virtual teaching assistant (vTA) powered by a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) model using OpenAI's conversational agent technology. vTA was built as an AI (Artificial Intelligence) powered chatbot and was used in two graduate-level online cybersecurity courses: Risk Management and Data Privacy. It answers questions on course materials and helps support the student users with the online teaching & learning process, including online correspondence related to the class topics, materials and assignments. We describe the vTA system architecture, as well as the student usage patterns, and gather feedback plus learner experience data from 96 graduate students across the 2 cybersecurity classes through an online survey questionnaire. Our collected survey data indicates that participating students found vTA to be helpful as a supporting medium. Additionally, we analyze the student interactions with vTA using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to uncover key themes and to assess how well student questions aligned with the vTA system's responses. Based on the promising results obtained through this preliminary pilot study, we determine the efficacy and impact of vTA integrated online instruction. To our knowledge, this is one of the first applications of a RAG-powered virtual teaching assistant in cybersecurity education, and it is also a first-of-its-kind study on the utilization of an AI chatbot in cybersecurity teaching as a supplementary aid in distance learning mode. Our overall findings suggest that AI chatbots, like vTA, can meaningfully enhance learning in online cybersecurity education.

title: How Machines Learn to Decide When to Alarm: A Case Study on Temperature Control
authors: Amir Hossein Baradaran, Mahdi Naghibi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Temperature control is a critical factor in many industries, including the plastics and packaging industries, where precise thermal management is essential for product quality and operational safety. This highlights the importance of temperature control systems and alarm mechanisms to notify operators when conditions deviate from acceptable ranges. The application of intelligent methods in such industries can significantly enhance control accuracy and operational reliability. Despite this, no prior studies have investigated the use of machine learning to learn the behavior of a Kiefel machine’s temperature control system. This article presents an innovative approach in which machine learning algorithms are trained to replicate the on/off behavior of a temperature controller. Various supervised learning models, including Naive Bayes, Support Vector Machines (SVM) with different kernels, Logistic Regression, and k-Nearest Neighbors, were evaluated and compared. Results show that the SVM with a radial basis function (RBF) kernel achieved the highest performance, reaching an accuracy of 99.94%. The model’s performance was further analyzed using additional evaluation metrics, confirming its reliability in accurately learning and reproducing the temperature control behavior. This study highlights the potential of machine learning to model industrial control logic and establishes a foundation for intelligent, data-driven control and alarm systems.

title: Live Learning Methodology: Its Impact on Satisfaction, Perceived Usefulness, and Competency Development in Engineering
authors: Rosalino Rodríguez-Calderón, Alejandro Martínez-Borquez, Roberto Rosas-Rangel
abstract: In this paper, the implementation of a living learning methodology in the course “Engineering and Science Modeling” at Tecnológico de Monterrey is presented. This course is distinguished by integrating academic and extracurricular elements, providing flexible, collaborative, and open experiences. As a case study, two activities were carried out according to the methodology, one in a public recreational area and the other in a science fair format. The results of the qualitative/quantitative survey (Likert scale of 1 to 10) demonstrated outstanding success of these activities, with average scores above <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.5 / 10$</tex> and 90 % positive comments in the sentiment analysis. Additionally, the correlation analysis validated the central premise of the study by finding a positive and robust association between student satisfaction and their perception of usefulness and the development of professional competencies. In conclusion, this dynamic methodology generated a highly satisfactory learning experience, which was directly correlated with the perceived achievement of academic objectives, confirming its effectiveness in linking students' emotional engagement with learning outcomes.

title: LuzIA: Transforming University Self-Study with Artificial Intelligence
authors: Delia Aurora Galván Sánchez, Maritza Peña-Becerril, Claudia Camacho-Zuñiga
abstract: Amid the fast dissemination of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into educational institutions, instead of a polarized viewpoint, either hype-AI or hypo-AI, this innovative work proposes an ethically balanced human-AI pedagogical model. Our research demonstrates how a pedagogically guided generative AI tutor, such as LuzIA, can support self-study processes in higher education while preserving human instructional authority. The study followed a quasi-experimental design involving two groups-experimental <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=30)$</tex> and control <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=29)$</tex>-of undergraduate business students. The intervention was implemented in a Mathematics Reasoning course in August-December 2024. Through a mixed-methods approach, we analyzed the differences in average final-test scores as well as students' responses to a Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) questionnaire. The findings suggest design requirements for these tools and highlight their potential to provide flexible and personalized support without overburdening the instructor. Furthermore, students' responses reframe technology acceptance as a function of learning efficacy rather than usability alone.

title: Effect of Semiconductor Surface Characteristics & Cleaning Chemicals on Contact Angle Measurement
authors: Tatt Wai Wan, Pei Li Ooi, Yong Sheng Chong, Masrina Mohd Nadzir
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Contact angle (CA) measurement is a powerful technique for characterizing material surface properties. However, the typical application of Goniometer in semiconductor manufacturing is on blank wafer surface wetting analysis. In this study, a customized CA measurement setup is developed to measure CA on structured wafers with different chips’ density. With the aid of ImageJ software, the CA of the droplet images taken from the setup is analysed. The setup demonstrated an increase in CA on structured wafers from low, mid to high chips’ density due to the higher density of scrap lines. Lower CA is also observed on implanted silicon (Si) surface. After the cleaning with 10% H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>, all these wafers show further reduction of CA due to the removal of contaminants. The setup also demonstrated that the lowest CA of the structured wafer can be achieved when the cleaning chemicals combinations include diluted hydrofluoric acid (DHF). Lower CA is preferred during the wafer cleaning process not only due to the higher hydrophilic surface that favoured the wetting of chemicals but also the reduction in the affinity of bubbles on the wafers. Higher or longer affinity of bubbles on the wafer surface will result in wafer floating or ineffective cleaning during the wet process, which should be avoided in any other times.

title: Analysis of Resistance Performance of High Dose Boron Doping
authors: Chea Hui Lee, Bohesharvind N. Murthi, Hank Chen, Wan Chun Zhang, Leonard M. Rubin
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Thanks to their flexibility, single wafer implanters, such as the Axcelis Purion H200 are capable of delivering device electrical performance comparable to conventional multiple wafer machines, despite different machines having different beamlines and scanning systems. In this work, we study the impact of dose rate of a very high dose p-type implantation, boron 15 keV 8.50×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">15</sup> ions/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> on a polysilicon layer and compare the sheet resistance between a single wafer high current implanter, Axcelis Purion H200, and a 13-wafer batch high current implanter, Axcelis GSD200E<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Different energies, dose, beam current, chiller temperature, implant mode and vertical scan speed are implanted on the Purion H200 on this low energy/high dose polysilicon layer to match the sheet resistance performance of the batch tool. On the Purion H200, the energy trim factor (deceleration) has been selected for a better polysilicon sheet resistance performance matching with minimal recipe modification to reduce logistical efforts.

title: Sustainable Lean-Based Integrated Optimization for Urban Waste Sweeping with Non-Motorized and Motorized Fleets and Operational Constraints
authors: Milad Rahimi, Javad Rezaeian, Iraj Mahdavi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Municipal solid waste collection and management constitute one of the fundamental challenges facing contemporary urban environments, which has gained increasing complexity due to population growth and escalating waste generation rates. This research presents an integrated mathematical model for optimizing municipal solid waste collection processes utilizing heterogeneous motorized and non-motorized fleet configurations while incorporating real-world operational constraints. The proposed model simultaneously addresses location allocation, routing, scheduling, and resource allocation problems. The primary innovation of this study lies in the consideration of perishable wet waste degradation, lean concepts, restricted time windows, priority-based street cleaning operations, and concurrent utilization of motorized and non-motorized fleet systems. The model encompasses six objective functions: minimization of total costs, maximization of collection efficiency, maximization of accumulation facility quality, maximization of collection priorities, minimization of waste spoilage, and minimization of idle capacity and delays. The NSGA-III metaheuristic algorithm was employed for problem resolution. Results from five numerical examples with varying difficulty levels demonstrate that the proposed model achieves up to 18% cost reduction, 23% efficiency improvement, and 15% waste spoilage reduction compared to conventional approaches. Sensitivity analysis reveals that waste spoilage rate exerts the most significant impact on system performance.

title: Anode Screen Effect on the Electroplated Nickel Layer
authors: Sanjoy Paul, Vilmarie Soler, John Pennacchia, John Garant, Brian O’Hara, Elizabeth Karapetyan, Edward Swinnich
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper presents a development work to reduce foreign material (FM) counts and improve yield. The FM (>50 μm) originated from the nickel (Ni) anodes in the electroplating process. To resolve this FM issue, a Ni anode screen was introduced in the Ni electroplating bath in front of the anodes. To compare the Ni plating process, we processed wafers with and without the Ni anode screen for stress, grain structure, and plated thickness analysis. A significant FM reduction was observed with similar stress level. However, we observed a 4x plated height increase with the Ni anode screen when compared to the process without the Ni anode screen. A possible reason could be the change in current density due to the screen incorporation and Ni grain structure.

title: A Framework for Critical AI Literacy in Engineering Education: Preparing Students for an Algorithmic Future
authors: Lydia Velázquez-García, Antonio Cedillo-Hernández, Roberto Martínez-Román
abstract: The accelerating addition of artificial intelligence (AI) into engineering practice and education demands more than technical proficiency—it requires a literate, critical mindset capable of navigating algorithmic systems, ethical dilemmas, and socio-technical transformations. This paper presents a conceptual framework for Critical AI Literacy (CAL) tailored to engineering education. Based on contemporary research in AI literacy, data ethics, and engineering education, the framework delineates four interrelated dimensions: Awareness, Technical Fluency, Ethical Agency, and Critical Reflection, and includes them in educational pathways for addition into undergraduate engineering programs. By shifting from an instrumental view of AI competence (i.e., “how to use” AI) to a reflective, agency-centered orientation (i.e., “how to understand and question” AI), the proposed CAL framework offers educators and program designers a structured lens for developing engineers who are not only technically capable but also ethically and critically aware. Implications for curriculum design, faculty development, and future research are discussed. As a conceptual contribution, this paper is intended as a foundational proposition for future empirical validation and development.

title: Implementation and Testing of B-Dot Algorithm on CubeSat 2U Nanosatellite Prototype
authors: Andrei Spatari, Vladimir Melnic, Maxim Kapusteanski, Alexei Martîniuc
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents the design and testing of an attitude determination and control system (ADCS) for a 2U CubeSat prototype, using magnetorquers and the B-dot algorithm. The choice of this solution is motivated by the low power consumption and low mass, compared to reaction wheels. The proposed system integrates hardware components (STM32 microcontroller, MPU9250 sensor, communication and data storage modules) and a software architecture capable of implementing the B-dot algorithm for angular velocity reduction and satellite orientation. Experimental results show that the algorithm enables the stabilization and reorientation of the prototype, but with a relatively long response time, caused by the low magnetic moment of the coils. The study highlights the feasibility of the solution, but also the need to optimize the magnetorquers design to improve performance.

title: Innovative Generative AI-Resistant Assessment Methods for Engineering Education in the AI Era
authors: Khaled Shaaban
abstract: The use of advanced generative AI presents a significant challenge to the integrity and effectiveness of traditional engineering assessments. This work addresses this challenge by proposing various assessment methods designed to be resistant to AI use. These methods change the focus from productbased and text-heavy outputs to process-oriented and competencybased evaluations. These methods are built on concepts emphasizing hands-on creation, real-time performance, reflection, and complex interpersonal skills. The strategies range from inclass practical exams and project-based learning to oral examinations, peer reviews, and design reviews. The different methods outlined in this work provide a clear pathway forward, demonstrating that the most effective defense against generative AI is not more sophisticated detection, but more human-centered pedagogy. By focusing assessments on uniquely human skills such as hands-on creation, real-time adaptation, and ethical reasoning, engineering instructors can not only maintain academic integrity but also more effectively foster the professional competencies required of future engineers.

title: Table of Authors
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)

title: A Mean-Value Performability Assessment of IoMT Systems Based-on Stochastic Reward Net and Fuzzy Lz-transform Method
authors: Moraru Victor, Guțuleac Emilian, Alexei Anatol, Gribincea Dorin, Cuzmin Simion, Seniușin Anton
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents a predictive approach for uncertainty mean-value performability modeling and assessments framework of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) System (IoMTS) based-on the Stochastic Reward Nets with Fuzzy parameters (FSRNs) and Fuzzy Lz-transform method (FLz-TM). This framework allows the development of compact models and efficient evaluation of the availability and cost security impact in which is used a proactive defense time-based Moving Target Defense technique. The application of FSRN and FLz-TM considerably improve computational efficiency of performability analysis, avoiding the curse of IoMT system states dimensionality. A numerical case study for mean-value performability analysis of a particular IoMT is given to show the effectiveness of proposed approach.

title: Experimental Evaluation of Planar Inductors for Application in Buck-Type DC-DC Converters
authors: Olimpian T. Paulet, Dorin O. Neacsu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of using planar inductors within the functionality of a Buck converter, utilizing the EVAL_TDA38826_1VOUT evaluation platform. Two construction topologies for planar inductors are analyzed, the circular geometry topology and the rectangular geometry topology, both in forced continuous conduction mode at various switching frequencies. The experimental section examines the waveform shapes for the converter's output voltage and the switching node specific to the switching transistor. The study discusses the results obtained from testing the planar inductors under identical operating conditions and, finally, provides conclusions based on the results obtained.

title: Impact of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization on Banknote Recognition
authors: Nima Sharifinia, Mohammad Javad Parseh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The detection of old and worn banknotes is crucial for banking automation and counterfeit prevention. Traditional methods rely on machine learning models, which require large datasets and high computational resources. This study proposes an efficient image-processing-based approach for detecting worn banknotes without machine learning. The method employs grayscale histograms, HSV histograms, and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to enhance contrast and extract wear-related features such as discoloration, faded ink, and creases. We further introduce a novel grid-based evaluation mechanism with Best-Match Voting and Thresholding to localize key regions and reduce misclassification. Experimental results show that combining CLAHE with the grid mechanism ensures reliable detection while maintaining computational efficiency, making the approach suitable for real-time automated banking systems.

title: A Robust LinUCB for Optimal User Selection in Fatigue-Aware Demand Response
authors: Hongrui Chen, Yuanshi Zhang, Xiaojing Qi, Yan Li, Rushuai Han, Weiqi Pan, Manshang Wang, Xiankai Sun
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Optimal user selection in demand response (DR) programs is challenging due to the non-stationary compliance behaviors of residential users. Existing learning-based dispatch algorithms primarily focus on expected response rates, leaving them vulnerable to extreme tail-risk defaults induced by cumulative user fatigue. This paper formulates the DR target tracking problem as a partially observable Markov decision process and proposes the Robust LinUCB algorithm. To overcome the structural model misspecification of linear estimation, an empirical Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) metric is introduced to precisely quantify historical prediction residuals. Furthermore, a dynamic penalty mechanism is developed by embedding real-time fatigue as an exponential multiplier into the CVaR evaluation. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly minimizes tracking regret and successfully prevents systemic fatigue breakdowns under various operational pressures.

title: Calculation Method for Induced Voltage in Non-Fully Parallel Lines
authors: Chenglin Liu, Caibo Liao, Xianfa Tian, Wanqing Wang, Jinbo Li, Hongliang Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate assessment of induced voltage in non-fully parallel transmission lines is crucial for operational safety. Traditional methods are challenged by non-uniform electromagnetic coupling in such lines due to their complex spatial configurations. A calculation method based on an improved two-dimensional equivalent model is therefore proposed. Conventional approaches are constrained by either complex modeling requirements or limited applicability to fully parallel lines. Through finite element simulations, the influence of spatial geometric parameters on electromagnetic coupling was investigated. Subsequently, an improved two-dimensional equivalent model incorporating correction factors was developed. The proposed method requires no complex modeling procedures. It can be implemented by modifying capacitance and inductance matrices based on traditional calculations for fully parallel lines. Applications include non-parallel configurations, cross-spanning conditions, and various grounding scenarios. In case validation, calculation errors were determined as 2.4% for electrostatic induced voltage and 5% for electromagnetic induced voltage. The accuracy and practical applicability of the method were confirmed. This provides reliable technical references for safety evaluations during de-energized maintenance operations.

title: Day-ahead Robust Pricing Strategy for Load Aggregators Considering User Response Uncertainty
authors: Jialu Li, Yixue Fan, Yanzhe Ren, Rui An, Yuxiong Huang, Zhaohong Bie
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: As load-side resources are increasingly recognized as vital components for enhancing power system flexibility, their integration into market-clearing and settlement frameworks has become a critical priority. A Robust Differentiated Pricing strategy is proposed in this paper to address the stochastic perturbations of end-users and the truncation-based assessment risks encountered during the execution phase of electricity markets. Although Load Aggregators (LAs) are established as essential intermediaries for managing fragmented demand-side resources, they are subjected to significant profit risks arising from unpredictable user behavior and stringent penalties for response deviations. To mitigate these risks, the power deviation characteristics of heterogeneous resources—specifically Interruptible Loads (IL), Directly-Controlled Loads (DLC), and Transferred Loads (TL)—are extracted through Monte Carlo sampling. A stochastic-informed robust pricing framework is subsequently developed to maximize the comprehensive revenue of the LA by optimizing differentiated internal incentive prices. It is demonstrated through simulation results that response redundancy is effectively induced by the proposed R-DP scheme by shifting the response center toward a secure median range within the assessment interval. Consequently, settlement security and profit stability are significantly enhanced, and the uncertainties of user responses in complex market environments are successfully hedged.

title: UC-Secure Multi-Factor Authentication with Dynamic Password Recovery and Fine-Grained Access Control_supp1-3700850.pdf
authors: Ding Wang
abstract: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) has been widely applied in various fields, including smart homes, autonomous driving, and mobile communication. Although a number of MFA schemes with different security goals and properties have been proposed, most of them are found to pay little attention to password forgetting and loss issues, which may lead to the permanent loss of the account. Additionally, little effort has been devoted to designing MFA schemes with fine-grained access control to perform authentication flexibly. Therefore, the above issues raise the question of “ how to construct a MFA scheme with dynamic password recovery and fine-grained access control?”. In this paper, we, for the first time, introduce attributes and a dynamic password recovery method to propose a multi-factor authentication scheme with dynamic password recovery and fine-grained control, named MFA-DPRF. In MFA-DPRF, authentication succeeds only when a user provides both a valid password and a set of attributes satisfying the specified access policy, thereby enabling fine-grained access control. Particularly, flexible access control can be achieved by verifying if user’s attributes meet the specific access policy. Furthermore, a dynamic password recovery method based on secret questions and the secret sharing technique has been designed to address password forgetting and loss issues. Users can not only recover the original password, but also update secret values such as security questions to enhance security. The security of MFA-DPRF can be reduced to the computational Diffie-Hellman problem under the Random Oracle Model. In addition, we analyze the security of MFA-DPRF in the universally composable (UC) framework, which ensures desirable properties such as composable security. The informal analysis proves that MFA-DPRF is secure against known attacks. Compared with the state-of-the-art works, performance analysis shows that MFA-DPRF is superior in security and efficiency.

title: Proactive Federated Anomaly Detection in Consumer Electronic Ecosystems via Adaptive Learning and Lightweight Auditability
authors: Chien-Ming Chen, Kailian Chai, Zahid Akhtar, Seema Agrawal, Rahul Kumar, Yachao Li
abstract: Consumer electronic ecosystems, such as smart vehicles, wearable devices, connected homes, and Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled transportation systems, face increasing cyber threats, privacy risks, and reliability challenges. Although federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, its deployment in such environments is still hindered by statistical heterogeneity, hyperparameter sensitivity, and limited training auditability. This paper proposes SABFL, a federated intrusion detection framework for consumer electronic ecosystems. It combines FedProx-based local training, online simulated annealing for adaptive hyperparameter tuning, and lightweight blockchain-assisted audit logging. The framework improves convergence stability under heterogeneous data and provides traceable training records with low additional overhead. Experimental results on the CICIDS2017 dataset show that SABFL achieves performance comparable to existing methods under IID conditions, while yielding significantly stronger robustness under non-IID scenarios. In highly heterogeneous settings, SABFL improves the Macro-F1 score by up to 20.58% compared with standard federated baselines, and outperforms the advanced FedNova approach by 16.43%. These results demonstrate the practicality of SABFL for proactive intrusion detection in distributed consumer electronic environments.

title: Person-in-WiFi 3D: Unified Model for 3D WiFi Perception
authors: Bo Qian, Xing Wei, Kangwei Yan, Han Ding, Jinsong Han, Fei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: WiFi signals, in contrast to cameras, offer privacy protection and occlusion resilience for some practical scenarios such as smart homes, elderly care, and virtual reality. Recent years have seen remarkable progress in the estimation of single-person 2D pose, single-person 3D pose, multi-person 2D pose, and single-person mesh. This paper takes a step forward by introducing Person-in-WiFi 3D, a pioneering WiFi system that accomplishes multi-person 3D human perception. Person-in-WiFi 3D has two main updates. Firstly, compared to previous systems that used only one WiFi transmitter and one WiFi receiver, it has a greater number of WiFi devices, which enhances its capability for capturing 3D spatial reflections from multiple individuals. This allows it to respond more flexibly to complicated 3D perception scenarios, achieving a breakthrough from 2D to 3D. Secondly, it utilizes a DETR-like architecture based on Hungarian matching to achieve the end-to-end estimation. It employs a hierarchical refinement strategy from coarse to fine, effectively utilizing features at the global level, human-instance level, and keypoint level, thereby achieving finer-grained estimation and reconstruction. On the basis of these designs, Person-in-WiFi 3D is storage-efficient, accurate, and fast compared to its predecessor. Wedeployed a proof-of-concept system in 4m × 3.5m areas and collected a multi-person human perception dataset Wiception3D of over 97K frames with seven volunteers, encompassing diverse scenes and multi-person scenarios. Wiception3D includes nearly 98k training samples and nearly 8k test samples, annotated for 3D pose estimation and 3D mesh reconstruction tasks. Person-in-WiFi 3D achieves a keypoint localization error of 93mm in 3D pose estimation and 41mm in mesh reconstruction, comparable to cameras and millimeter-wave radars. The project page is at https://aiotgroup.github.io/Person-in-WiFi-3D

title: Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter for Robust Cellular/MEMS-INS Localization
authors: Xumin Feng, Tian Jin, Honglei Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)-denied urban environments, cellular/microelectromechanical system-inertial navigation system (MEMS-INS) integrated positioning provides a promising solution for continuous navigation. However, in such environments, signal propagation is severely affected by multipath, blockage, and environmental dynamics, leading to heterogeneous measurement uncertainties, including time-varying noise, impulsive outliers, and persistent NLOS biases, which are difficult to handle within conventional estimation frameworks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter (VB-JMANIF) for robust positioning. The proposed method explicitly models NLOS-induced biases, impulsive outliers, and time-varying measurement noise, and jointly integrates anomaly classification, bias estimation, noise adaptation, and state update within an error-state cubature Kalman filter (CKF) framework. Simulation results demonstrate that VB-JMANIF consistently outperforms the conventional CKF and several state-of-the-art robust and noise-adaptive methods in terms of positioning accuracy, estimation stability, and robustness. Field experiments using real cellular signals further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, achieving horizontal positioning Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) values of 4.7 m and 6.1 m under moderate and severely degraded urban measurement conditions, respectively, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability in real-world environments.

title: A Compact and Reliable ACT Gate Driving Scheme Based on Active Clamp Paradigm
authors: Fucheng Liu, Jinpeng Wu, Chunpin Ren, Hui Liu, Han Wang, Lin Dong, Xiangyu Zhao, Qingtao Meng, Jiapeng Liu
abstract: The advent of Avalanche Controlled Thyristor (ACT) has addressed the shortage of the turn-off capability of Integrated Gate Commutated Thyristor (IGCT), establishing it as a promising candidate for DC circuit breaker (DCCB). However, its practical application is constrained by the complexity of the gate driver, which conventionally necessitates an additional blocking module for the blocking state. This article proposes a novel ACT gate driving scheme based on active clamp paradigm. By reconfiguring the turn-off module to provide the blocking function, the proposed topology eliminates the need for an additional blocking module. A 4.5 kV ACT prototype was developed to validate the scheme. Experimental results confirm that the proposed driver maintains same functionalities of conventional ACT. Furthermore, the proposed scheme reduces the driver footprint by 18% and static power loss by 15% while offering a more favorable reliability basis than the conventional scheme. These attributes position the proposed scheme as a promising solution for DCCB applications.

title:
DP
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    O: Dual-Pair Direct Preference Optimization for Preference-Guided Adversarial Robustness in Vision Models
authors: Ahmed Dawod Mohammed Ibrahum, Jang-Eui Hong
abstract: Adversarial training remains the most effective empirical defense against adversarial examples, yet it is hindered by the high cost of inner maximization, the clean–robust accuracy trade-off, and the underutilization of information contained in misclassified or near-boundary adversarial samples.We propose DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>O, a preference-guided framework that converts adversarial evaluations into structured preference pairs and operationalizes Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for vision robustness. Specifically, we are the first to import the implicit-reward, reference-anchored DPO formulation into the adversarial robustness setting for vision models, andwe do so through a dual-pool construction in which adversarial failures formunstable pairs (U-pairs) preferring the ground-truth label over the model’s incorrect prediction, while adversarial successes form resilient pairs (R-pairs) preferring the ground-truth label over the runner-up class. A hybrid objective then combinesDPOon these typed pairs with cross-entropy on resilient examples.Across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10 using ResNet-18 and a diverse attack suite (FGSM, PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8,20</sub>, PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">64,40</sub>, EOTPGD, Jitter, andAutoAttack), DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Oconsistently improves adversarial robustness over strong baselines while maintaining competitive clean accuracy. On CIFAR-10, the hybrid model improves PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8,20</sub> robustness from 0.4853 (TRADES) to 0.5846, increases FGSM robustness to 0.6718, and achieves 0.4934 under AutoAttack while maintaining 0.9125 clean accuracy. Similar gains are observed on CIFAR-100 and STL-10, where DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>O improves robustness across both iterative and adaptive attacks. These results demonstrate that reframing adversarial robustness as preference learning effectively reuses adversarial outcomes as supervision and improves the clean–robust trade-off without requiring additional critic models.

title: AI Driven Multimodal Arrival Time Prediction for Urban Autorickshaw and Bus Networks Using GPS Telemetry and Demand–Supply Analytics
authors: Sundarakrishnan Narayanan, Tanmay Umesh, Sohan Varier, Tarun Bhupathi, Mohana
abstract: This paper presents a unified system for predicting urban travel times across buses and auto-rickshaws in Bengaluru, with an emphasis on real-time accuracy and smooth multimodal travel. The work covers three linked tasks: forecasting when a bus will reach its upcoming stops, estimating auto-rickshaw ride durations, and combining both to compute end-to-end auto–bus–auto journeys. Thisworkwas developed as a part of The Bengaluru Last Mile Challenge 2025 in collaboration with the Indian Institute of Science, BMTC and Namma Yatri.The bus component processes large volumes of GPS telemetry across 7509 BMTC bus routes in the city. A sequence-modeling approach based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with an attention module, is used to learn the temporal movement patterns from historical trajectories, and predicted arrival times are further refined using Kalman Filtering to smooth temporal inconsistencies. Using a fixed sequence length of 60 with a horizon of 6, the model captures short-term dynamics while remaining efficient for large-scale deployment. The system achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 85.84 seconds for bus arrival prediction tackling real world challenges such as Noisy GPS signals and route deviations. The auto-rickshaw module estimates acceptance time, pickup delay, and in-ride travel time using demand–supply patterns, the street network, and time-varying speed data. Via comparison to real life events, the module was able to achieve and error of ~0.54 minutes for acceptance time, ~1.55 minutes for pickup time and ~1.75 minutes for journey time predictions. These two modules are then fused by running one after another, to capture waiting times, transfer reliability, and day-to-day traffic fluctuations. The overall framework is designed to be interpretable, efficient, and resilient to noisy or incomplete data, making it suitable for real-time city-scale deployment. The resulting system is used to predict real-time multimodal travel time estimation for first and last mile integrated journeys, supporting intelligent transport systems, multimodal route planning, and urban mobility analytics.

title: A Simplified Dual-Output Power Conversion Scheme Based on Efficient Utilization of Resonant Forward-Flyback Topology
authors: Ting Qian, Chen Jin
abstract: This paper proposes a simple dualoutput scheme based on efficient utilization of resonant forward-flyback topology with adjustable resonant time. Specifically, the new structure adds an extra output port for the resonant forward by properly utilizing the flyback mode of the magnetic reset winding, thereby achieving independent control for two isolated outputs in a low-cost manner. Meanwhile, considering the limited adjustment range of resonant forward and the need for a preceding cascade circuit, this scheme couples the two stages by using resonant capacitor to support the intermediate bus. This enables the resonance to dynamically match the energy transfer time of forward portion for reduced conduction losses, and thus, allows the flyback portion to reduce switching losses by simultaneously adjusting switching frequency and duty cycle. Based on detailed theoretical analysis, experimental results verify effectiveness of the proposed concept.

title: Market-Aware Asymmetric Loss Framework for Day-Ahead Building Energy Demand Forecasting in the Electricity Market
authors: Hyeongyu Son, Musu Kim, Younggyu Hwang, Sekyung Han
abstract: In electricity markets with two-settlement structures, forecast errors carry asymmetric economic consequences: under-prediction forces costly real-time procurement at prices 15-50% above day-ahead levels during peak hours, while over-prediction incurs milder opportunity costs of 4-6% of day-ahead prices. Despite this fundamental asymmetry, most building energy forecasting models optimize symmetric metrics such as MAPE and RMSE producing statistically unbiased but economically suboptimal predictions. Moreover, the degree of cost asymmetry is not constant but varies dramatically with time of day, yet existing asymmetric approaches apply fixed penalty ratios uniformly across all hours. This paper proposes a Market-Aware Asymmetric Loss (MAL) framework that bridges this gap between forecasting training objectives and market settlement realities. The core innovation is a Time-Dependent Quantile (TD-τ) mechanism that adapts prediction conservativeness hourly based on empirically-derived cost asymmetry ratios: τ = 0.70 during on-peak hours when under-prediction is 3.5 times costlier, relaxing to τ = 0.55 during off-peak hours when costs are nearly symmetric. The framework further integrates building pattern classification for strategy selection, a seven-component SMP simulator validated against 8,760 hourly KPX observations across 12 statistical metrics (mean values within 2%, distributional properties within 8%), and comprehensive uncertainty quantification. As a proof-of-concept study, experiments are conducted on two Korean buildings with contrasting consumption profiles (a high-variability commercial office and a regular-pattern residential complex) using 3-fold time-series cross-validation. The framework’s principal contribution is a substantial reduction in under-prediction settlement cost—the economically dominant error mode: up to 50.4% on the commercial building and 41.9% on the residential building in controlled ablation against a symmetric (RMSE-only) baseline. Relative to the symmetric baseline, the full framework further yields up to 16.3% total MAPE improvement and 28.5% settlement cost reduction for the high-variability commercial building (single-split ablation), and a 3.6% aggregate MAPE reduction in cross-validation. Statistical significance of the full-framework versus symmetric-baseline comparison is confirmed by the Diebold-Mariano test (DM=−4.37, p<0.001). Against a well-tuned fixed-quantile baseline, TD-τ achieves comparable aggregate accuracy (MAPE differences under one percent in both buildings); its added value is theoretical consistency with the Granger-optimal quantile result, per-hour interpretability, and automatic adaptability to structural changes in the hourly cost-asymmetry ratio. The benefit is strongly moderated by building type: substantial on high-variability commercial buildings and marginal on stable residential patterns, providing deployment guidance for practitioners.

title: Caf4AVC: LLM-Enhanced Collaborative Framework for Attribute Value Canonicalization in Open KBs
authors: Ying He, Qiang Yang, Yaxin Wang, Dan Gao, Zhouhong Gu, Zhixu Li, Yanghua Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Open Knowledge Bases (Open KBs) are fundamental to knowledge-driven applications, including semantic search, knowledge reasoning, and recommendation systems. However, the presence of redundant and ambiguous expressions within Open KBs significantly hinders their application. This highlights the urgent need for Open KB canonicalization, particularly of attribute values, which comprise nearly 40% of the facts within Open KBs. Unlike entities and predicates, attribute values are inherently sparse and diverse, posing unique challenges for their canonicalization. However, existing studies mainly focus on entities or predicates, leaving attribute value-level noun phrase canonicalization (NPC-AV) underexplored. Large language models (LLMs), with their strengths in common-sense reasoning and fault tolerance, have shown promise in Open KB canonicalization. Yet, current LLM-based approaches often rely heavily on LLM responses, overlooking their high computational cost and potential errors. In this paper, we introduce Caf4AVC, a collaborative framework that integrates clustering-based methods and LLMs for the NPC-AV task. We further propose an innovative two-factor authentication correction mechanism and an adaptive threshold-based selection strategy to address these limitations. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world Open KB datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, achieving a 17.52% reduction in LLM call costs and a 6.3% average performance improvement compared to competitive methods. The code and dataset are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/hedyHe/Caf4AV</uri>.

title: Post-Die-Bond Defect Detection in Semiconductor Packaging AOI Under Extreme Data Scarcity: Copy-Paste Augmentation and Leakage-Aware Evaluation
authors: Der-Fa Chen, Yanyu Lai, Chih-Chung Wang
abstract: In this study, we target the early-production bootstrap stage of an automatic optical inspection system, where a new inspection station or production line has started collecting data but true defect cases remain extremely scarce.We evaluated same-station defect localization at an inspection station between die bonding and wire bonding in a semiconductor packaging line. After exact image-level deduplication, the dataset contained 135 images, including 21 defective images and 30 annotated boxes. Because one physical defect could appear in more than one camera view, these annotations corresponded to 19 independent physical defects across 14 die locations. The practical question is whether a first useful defect-localization model can be built before enough defective samples accumulate for conventional supervised training. The experiments used a standard one-stage detector, offline Copy-Paste augmentation, and leakage-aware grouped cross-validation that kept repeated views of the same physical defect in the same fold. Under three random seeds with five folds per seed, the raw baseline reached a mean Average Precision at 0.50 IoU (mAP50) of 0.563, while the CP80× setting reached 0.957 with recall 0.935. As an anomaly-detection reference, PatchCore reached an image-level area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.977 ± 0.020 for separating defect-free and defective images, but it does not produce three-class bounding boxes. These results indicate that fold-local data construction and leakage-aware validation can make same-station early detector bootstrapping practical when defects are rare, while station-specific background regularity and the absence of cross-station validation remain external-validity limitations.

title: A Physically Interpretable Hybrid Framework for Spatial Modeling of Crowd-Modified Wet-Bulb Heat Risk During Mass Gatherings
authors: Fayçal Hamdi, Mohamed Benaida, Hachemi Bennaceur, Yasser Aldwyan, Hani Almoamari, Abdulaziz Alshahrani, Abdullah Alshanqiti
abstract: Extreme heat poses a critical risk in high-density mass gatherings, where atmospheric conditions, urban morphology, and crowd dynamics interact to produce hazardous microclimates. During the Hajj pilgrimage in Makkah, these effects are amplified by dense crowds, heat-retaining surfaces, and constrained ventilation. Conventional Urban Heat Island (UHI) indicators based on land surface temperature do not directly capture human thermophysiological stress and are therefore inadequate for operational heat-risk assessment. This paper introduces a physics-guided hybrid framework for modeling crowd-modified wet-bulb heat risk. The approach combines (i) wet-bulb temperature as a physiologically grounded baseline; (ii) a data-driven spatial amplification term derived from satellite-based morphological features; (iii) scenario-based estimates of crowd-induced heat contributions; and (iv) wind-related modulation that accounts for stagnation and dust effects. By explicitly separating time-varying atmospheric forcing from spatial susceptibility, the framework provides an interpretable decomposition of heat-risk drivers. The machine-learning model for estimating the spatial amplification component achieves a mean absolute error of 1.13 °C (R2 = 0.586). Application of the framework to the 2021–2025 Hajj seasons suggests distinct site-specific heat-risk regimes, with Arafat and Muzdalifah dominated by urban amplification and Haram and Mina more strongly influenced by crowd density. Across all years, the combined influence of urban morphology, crowd density, and wind conditions results in an average increase of approximately 2.1 °C to 2.3 °C in effective wet-bulb temperature across sites. The 2024 Hajj season is identified as the most severe, with a substantial increase in extreme-risk exposure. The proposed framework provides a spatially explicit, physically interpretable, and decision-support-oriented methodology for heat-risk assessment within the Hajj context. The decomposition-based framework is, in principle, transferable to other large-scale mass-gathering environments, subject to retraining of the spatial amplification model and replacement of the event-specific crowd allocation component with context-appropriate crowd estimation approaches.

title: SMART: Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking in Complex Motion Scenarios
authors: Zijing Zhao, Xiang Wei, Zhihui Li, Xiaojun Chang, Shunli Zhang
abstract: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) detects multiple objects in videos and assigns unique identities across consecutive frames, generating complete motion tracks. Existing methods typically associate fixed-size predicted tracks with detections above a threshold, but objects with complex motion patterns, such as sudden changes in speed or direction, often cause motion estimation errors, leading to missed associations and fragmented tracks. To tackle this, we analyze the impact of attributes like speed, size, and detection confidence on tracking performance. Based on this, we propose an adaptive scaling strategy that dynamically adjusts the size of predicted tracks to create adaptively scaled bounding boxes. These bounding boxes enable a Motion-Adaptive IoU (MA-IoU) association strategy for more robust matching between detections and predicted tracks. Additionally, we introduce a self-supervised training method to optimize the adaptive scaling network and MA-IoU across diverse scenarios. Finally, we cascade MA-IoU to develop SMART (Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking), a robust and efficient multi-object tracker. SMART achieves state-of-the-art results on BEE24 and DanceTrack. On the BEE24 benchmark, SMART achieves 67.2 MOTA, 63.4 IDF1, and 47.9 HOTA, demonstrating superior performance in handling objects with complex motion and indistinguishable appearances. This comprehensive approach addresses the limitations of prior methods, offering a significant advancement in multi-object tracking.

title: Sensorless Control of Dual Three-Phase PMSM Using a Subspace Decoupling-Based Fuzzy Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Observer
authors: Luhui Xu, Zhiguo Zhao, Peng Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: To address the chattering, parameter sensitivity, and limited operating-condition adaptability of conventional sliding mode observers (SMOs) in sensorless control of dual three-phase permanent magnet synchronous motors (DTP-PMSMs), this paper proposes a subspace-decoupling-based integrated method combining a fuzzy super-twisting sliding mode observer (F-STSMO) and grey wolf optimization (GWO). In the proposed method, fuzzy control is integrated with the super-twisting sliding mode observer (STSMO) to construct a fuzzy time-varying-gain observer. The stability of the observer under time-varying disturbances is demonstrated, which effectively suppresses chattering and improves the observation performance over a wide dynamic range. By exploiting the multiple degrees of freedom of DTP-PMSMs, a GWO-based parameter estimation algorithm is developed. The sensitivity to initial settings and the convergence conditions are discussed, enabling high-accuracy real-time motor parameter estimation and enhancing the robustness of the STSMO against parameter variations. Furthermore, the Lyapunov stability constraints of the integrated F-STSMO and GWO method are analyzed. Experimental results show that the proposed integrated F-STSMO and GWO sensorless control method achieves a resistance estimation error of 0.9% for the DTP-PMSM, reduces the rotor position error caused by parameter variations by 88.6%, and keeps the rotor position error fluctuation within 2.51° under both steady-state and dynamic operating conditions.

title: Short Time-scale Analysis of Dead-time Effect in Dual Active Half-Bridge Converter
authors: Su-Bin Kang, Hyeon-Sik Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In this article, the dead-time effects of a dual active half-bridge (DAHB) converter are analyzed on a short time scale. Dead time distorts the ac-side voltages of both bridges, leading to polarity reversal and phase drift. Consequently, power transfer characteristics depend not only on the phase-shift ratio and voltage ratio but also on the dead-time value. A detailed short time-scale analysis of the operating waveforms is presented over one switching cycle, incorporating various operating states such as buck, match, and boost conditions. From this analysis, precise power flow equations are derived, revealing that the power characteristics become non-monotonic and exhibit power plateau regions depending on the operating modes. Furthermore, a dead-time compensation algorithm is proposed to mitigate these distortions and linearize the output voltage control. Finally, the validity of the derived model is confirmed through simulation and experimental results under extensive power flow conditions.

title: Toward Inference Consistency: Polynomial-Driven Control Synthesis of Shared Steering Systems via Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Fatigue Monitoring
authors: Zhenyang Yan, Xiang-Peng Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This article is concerned about the driving performance of human-machine shared steering systems under different fatigue level of drivers. To mitigate risks arisen from driver fatigue, a novel framework featuring an interpretable driver fatigue inference mechanism coupled with a flexible gain scheduling control scheme is proposed. First, an interval type-2 fuzzy logic system (IT2 FLS) with Gaussian membership functions that adhere to interpretability constraints is employed to monitor the driver’s fatigue level. This design provides a reliable basis for dynamic authority allocation that sensitively perceives changes in fatigue level while maintaining inference consistency. Second, a polynomial-driven switching gain-scheduling (PDSGS) scheme is constructed for path-tracking assistance in the human-machine shared steering system. Driven by the homogeneous polynomials technique, the PDSGS scheme can achieve more flexible steering assistance responses with the integration of dynamic gain matching mechanism. Indeed, by systematically classifying the membership distribution into distinct subsets, gain matrices are switched in real time to improve shared steering performance. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by the driver-in-the-loop simulation platform.

title: Radio Frequency Fingerprinting: A Survey of AI Generalization Challenges and Solutions for Wireless Security
authors: Nordine Quadar, Abdellah Chehri, Benoit Debaque, Halim Yanikomeroglu, Gunes Karabulut Kurt
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Radio frequency (RF) fingerprinting has emerged as a promising physical-layer security technique for device authentication in wireless networks, offering protocol-independent identification by exploiting hardware-induced signal characteristics and imperfections. This paper explores its challenges and potential within the heterogeneous wireless networks, including UAV communications and IoT deployments which are characterized by dynamic operating conditions, heterogeneous device profiles, and elevated security demands. Although deep learning-based methods have substantially improved controlled recognition accuracy from 70–90% to as high as 95–99%, the generalization gap, identified as the severe performance degradation when deployment conditions differ from training environments, remains the fundamental barrier to reliable deployment in practical environments. Systems frequently demonstrate performance degradation, ranging from 30–70%, when confronted with unseen datasets and real-world operating conditions that differ from those encountered during training. This survey systematically traces the evolution of RF fingerprinting, from traditional manual feature engineering to cutting-edge learning architectures, with a particular emphasis on generalization challenges. Key research gaps are identified in multi-factor generalization modeling, real-world validation under diverse conditions, scalable architecture design for broad device deployments, adaptive learning mechanisms for dynamic contexts, and computational efficiency for resource-constrained platforms. The analysis provides foundational insight for advancing next-generation RF fingerprinting techniques capable of sustaining robust and consistent performance across highly variable and complex wireless environments. By clarifying current limitations and future directions, this work contributes to the practical realization of secure RF-based authentication for heterogeneous wireless networks.

title: Soft-Masked Transformer for Point Cloud Processing With Skip Attention-Based Upsampling
authors: Yong He, Hongshan Yu, Chaoxu Mu, Mingtao Feng, Tongjia Chen, Zechuan Li, Anwaar Ulhaq, Ajmal Mian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Point cloud processing methods leverage local and global point features to cater to downstream tasks, yet they often overlook the task-level context inherent in point clouds during the encoding stage. We argue that integrating task-level information into the encoding stage significantly enhances performance. To that end, we propose SMTransformer which incorporates task-level information into a vector-based transformer by utilizing a soft mask generated from task-level queries and keys to learn the attention weights. Additionally, to facilitate effective communication between features from the encoding and decoding layers in high-level tasks such as segmentation, we introduce a skip-attention-based up-sampling block. This block dynamically fuses features from various resolution points across the encoding and decoding layers. To mitigate the increase in network parameters and training time resulting from the complexity of the aforementioned blocks, we propose a novel shared point position encoding strategy. This strategy allows various transformer blocks to share the same position information over the same resolution points, thereby reducing network parameters and training time without compromising accuracy. Experimental comparisons with existing methods on multiple datasets demonstrate the efficacy of SMTransformer and skip-attention-based up-sampling for semantic segmentation task. In particular, we achieve state-of-the-art semantic segmentation results of 73.9% mIoU on S3DIS Area 5 and 62.4% mIoU on SWAN dataset. Note to Practitioners—Point cloud processing underpins automation tasks such as robotic perception, navigation, and inspection, where accurate 3D understanding is essential. Existing methods often prioritize vision benchmarks while overlooking automation needs like efficiency on limited hardware and robustness in real-world environments. The proposed SMTransformer embeds task-level guidance into feature learning and employs skip-attention up-sampling to improve segmentation accuracy with practical efficiency. It is well-suited for robotic manipulation, autonomous driving, and inspection applications. Current limitations include reliance on GPUs and sensitivity to extreme density variations. Future work will target edge-device deployment and multi-task extensions.

title: A System-Theoretic Approach to Hawkes Process Identification with Guaranteed Positivity and Stability
authors: Xinhui Rong, Girish N. Nair
abstract: The Hawkes process models self-exciting event streams, requiring a strictly non-negative and stable stochastic intensity. Standard identification methods enforce these properties using non-negative causal bases, yielding conservative parameter constraints and severely ill-conditioned least-squares Gram matrices at higher model orders. To overcome this, we introduce a system-theoretic identification framework utilizing the sign-indefinite orthonormal Laguerre basis, which guarantees a well-conditioned asymptotic Gram matrix independent of model order. We formulate a constrained least-squares problem enforcing the necessary and sufficient conditions for positivity and stability. By constructing the empirical Gram matrix via a Lyapunov equation and representing the constraints through a sum-of-squares trace equivalence, the proposed estimator is efficiently computed via semidefinite programming.

title: IEEE Internet of Things Journal Publication Information

title: DR-SFL: Dynamic and Resource-Aware Split Federated Learning for Semantic-Enabled 6G Industrial IoT Systems
authors: Saifur Rahman, Jeetasha Arora, Shantanu Pal, Chandan Karmakar
abstract: As 6G networks transition toward “Native AI”, Semantic Communication (SemCom) has emerged as a vital paradigm to reduce bandwidth by transmitting high-level features rather than raw data. Split Federated Learning (SFL) is a key enabler for this transition; however, static model partitioning often fails in heterogeneous 6G-IoT environments. We propose Dynamic Resource-aware Split Federated Learning (DR-SFL), a framework that optimises the semantic-level exchange between mobile clients and the cloud by dynamically adapting split points. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets demonstrate that DR-SFL significantly enhances convergence efficiency compared to traditional FedAvg and static SFL. Notably, in non-IID CIFAR-10 environments, DR-SFL reaches an 80% accuracy target in just 27–28 rounds, outperforming FedAvg, which requires 35 rounds. Furthermore, on the MNIST dataset, DR-SFL achieves a 98% target in as few as 3–4 rounds, compared to 6–8 rounds for FedAvg. System-level analysis reveals that the framework maintains high energy efficiency, with average battery drain as low as 0.44% for simple tasks and 6.45% for complex non-IID tasks. These results highlight DR-SFL as a robust solution for scalable, energy-efficient semantic intelligence in the evolving 6G-IoT ecosystem.

title: HMFM: A Robust Hierarchical Multimodal Fusion Module to Enhance Map Learning With Additional Information_supp1-3698777.pdf
authors: Shitao Chen
abstract: High-definition (HD) maps, rich in geometric and semantic information, are essential for autonomous driving systems. Recent online map learning methods, predicting maps directly from onboard sensor data, offer promising alternatives to traditional manual approaches. However, they are limited in long-range detection and complex environments due to onboard sensors’ inherent constraints. While some works incorporate external priors, they often lack generality due to tight coupling with specific architectures and poor robustness to imperfect data. To address these gaps, we propose the Hierarchical Multimodal Fusion Module (HMFM), a robust, generalizable plug-and-play module that fuses onboard data with external information at two levels. At the feature level, the Masked Attention Module (MAM) selectively enhances features to boost fusion efficiency and minimize interference. At the Bird’s Eye View (BEV) level, the Alignment Module (AM) resolves spatial discrepancies; then, the Unified Attention Module (UAM) adaptively weighs external priors to effectively mitigate inaccuracies from imperfect data. We also introduce a distillation framework to distill benefits from additional information into the model without affecting runtime efficiency. Extensive experiments validate HMFM’s generality, leveraging satellite and SD maps for significant gains across baselines (e.g., +22.0 mIoU on HDMapNet, +12.3 mAP on MapTR). Furthermore, the distillation framework boosts the original HDMapNet by 2.8 mIoU without affecting runtime efficiency. The code and data processing pipeline for this work are publicly available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/xjtu-cs-gao/HMFM</uri>
