It examines how these reforms enhance women’s participation, leadership, and decision-making power, while identifying persistent barriers such as patriarchal norms, proxy representation, and institutional gaps. The study also explores state-specific variations to highlight contextual influences on empowerment outcomes. By offering evidence-based policy recommendations, this research contributes to India’s gender equality framework and global discourse on women’s political agency, aligning with Sustainable Development Goal 5.

title: Real-Time Speech Translation for Mobile Communication Using AI-Based Text Recognition
authors: Mohammed Javaid Ul Islam, Mubeen Ahmed Khan, Owais Ahmad Shah, Abdul Haq Nalband, Ankita Chourasia
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: As technology increases the demand for smooth global communication, the need for language translation across various sectors, including travel, healthcare, education, and social work, is also increasing globally. As technology is increasing the language barrier with the process of NLP technology make it easier and the concept of language differences can still be easily disrupted for smooth communications in mobile platforms. This paper presents an AI-Powered best mobile solutions which breaks the barriers of language constraints with speech recognition, language identification, machine translation and successful speech synthesis within on a common platform. This platform is capable to handle more than 50 languages online as well as offline mode when downloaded with the supported language packs. This puts a strong emphasis on user data privacy. Instead of switching between different applications, this system allows uniquely a smooth conversation to a natural flow of response in one second. The testing shows that the system achieves a strong accuracy of translation with having BLEU score of more than 40 and user satisfaction levels exceeding 90%. This paper outlines the system design and its flutter-based user interface which is helpful in hybrid cloud edge architecture for discussion in social benefit and potential for scaling the speech translation technique.

title: Machine Learning-Based Information Processing for Smart Contact Lens Tear Glucose Sensing
authors: Zongkang Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th World Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (WCCCT)
abstract: Recent advances in soft electronics and Internet of Things (IoT) have accelerated the development of wearable devices for noninvasive health monitoring. Capable of monitoring vital signs in eyes and tear fluids, smart contact lenses have been considered one of the most promising next-generation wearable technologies. However, previously reported smart contact lenses rely heavily on embedded system-on-chips (SoCs) and wireless modules for information processing and data transmission, which significantly complicate fabrication processes for space-constrained applications. Here, we reported an intelligent tear glucose sensing method based on electrochromic materials. Glucose information can be directly informed by visible color changes, and quantitative results are processed through image processing and machine learning. The detection range of the resulting smart contact lens covers both ordinary and high glucose levels, with a high correlation coefficient r = 0.99543 and a lower detection limitation of 0.05 mM, while eliminating requirements for antennas, batteries, and complex integrated circuits.

title: Secure Consortium-Based Blockchain-IPFS Framework for Scalable Electronic Health Record Management
authors: Kusum Lata, Khyati Grover
publish: Proceedings of the 8th ACM International Symposium on Blockchain and Secure Critical Infrastructure
abstract: None

title: An End-to-End Deep Learning Pipeline for Automated Mandible Virtual Surgical Planning Using Real-World Clinical Data
authors: Nattapon Kamboonsri, Teejuta Sriwaranon, Natdanai Tantisereepatana, Chedtha Puncreobutr, Boonrat Lohwongwatana, Gregory B. Olson, Alessandro Tel, Massimo Robiony, Titipat Achakulvisut, Peerapon Vateekul
abstract: Virtual surgical planning (VSP) is essential step in mandible reconstruction, involving multiple tasks, such as structure segmentation, cutting-plane definition, and implant planning. While many studies have shown promising results on subtasks using specialized ML models, these models are typically developed in isolation. This fragmentation prevents their seamless integration into a unified, automated clinical pipeline. We present a fully automated VSP assistant integrated into 3D Slicer combining segmentation, reconstruction, and planning modules into a single clinical framework. The proposed pipeline was validated on real clinical datasets, including unhealthy mandible dataset containing mandible abnormalities, healthy mandible dataset with normal mandible, and fibula dataset of normal fibula. Quantitative evaluation using the dice similarity coefficient (DSC) yielded scores of 0.911 for mandible segmentation, 0.854 for defect segmentation, 0.908 for fibula segmentation, and 0.816 for mandible reconstruction. Planning module reduced average operation time by 57% while maintaining accuracy comparable to manual planning. End-to-end evaluation on real clinical cases demonstrated a dramatic reduction in the total time engineer spent completing tasks from average of 5 hours 21 minutes using the manual workflow to 29 minutes 38 seconds. Our results demonstrate that automated VSP offers a practical, clinically precise solution that reduces the time-intensive nature of VSP, bridging the gap between isolated ML research and real-world surgical practice.

title: Secure Federated Data Augmentation Empowered Efficient THz Semantic Communication for Agentic AI Collaborative Networking
authors: Zihong Li, Jinhao Zhou, Qianqian Pan, Jun Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) collaborative networking and secure federated learning are emerging as critical enablers for unmanned swarms with the advent of 6G and the proliferation of AI-enabled IoT devices, where autonomous driving and cooperative tasks require fast, reliable, and energy-efficient communication and learning. However, massive multimodal sensor data, the limited bandwidth of conventional mmWave V2X links, heterogeneous local datasets, privacy concerns over sensitive observations, and the strict energy budgets of UAVs, UUVs, and small robots create major bottlenecks for traditional federated learning. To address these challenges, we propose a cross-layer network framework integrating 6G THz-enabled efficient semantic communication with secure federated data augmentation for agentic AI collaboration. Semantic compression over short-range THz links reduces per-round transmitted payloads by extracting only task-relevant features, while semantic prototype-based data augmentation accelerates FL convergence by enriching non-IID local datasets without additional communication overhead; secure aggregation (SecAgg) further ensures that individual model updates are never exposed to the aggregation server. This co-design simultaneously reduces per-round transmission cost and the total number of rounds required for convergence, yielding a compound reduction in cumulative communication energy. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system significantly outperforms a conventional mmWave-based federated learning baseline in transmission latency, tail-latency reliability, learning convergence speed, global model accuracy, and cumulative communication energy, validating its effectiveness for energy-efficient and privacy-preserving agentic AI collaboration.

title: Leveraging a passive MRAM crossbar for hardware-in-the-loop and continual learning_supp1-3702001.pdf
authors: D. RAVELOSONA

title: Paving the Way for 6G – Radio Enhancements for Extended Reality in 5G-Advanced Release-19
authors: Stefano Paris, Klaus I. Pedersen, Ebin Chacko, Abolfazl Amiri, Claudio Rosa, Santiago Morejon, Zexian Li, Umar Saeed, Boyan Yanakiev
abstract: This article describes the latest advancements for real-time eXtended Reality (XR) communications over 3GPP 5G-Advanced Release-19, as well as the anticipated enhancements for the upcoming 6G radio design. Release-19 introduces two main categories of enhancements: those for better supporting delay-criticalXRtraffic, such as measurement gap skipping and uplink scheduling improvements like latency aware logical channel prioritization and enhancements to delay status reporting, which significantly increase system-levelXRcapacity; and application-awareness enablers, focusing on uplink rate control for congestion management via application rate adaptation and multi-modality enhancements. Looking ahead the 6G radio design is projected to further elevate XR performance through advanced physical layer and RAN protocol enhancements. System-level simulation results for 3.5GHz with 100MHz bandwidth demonstrate promising capacity increases compared to 5G, and the performance is expected to be further enhanced by utilizing new mid-bands, such as around 7GHz, with extreme MIMO and wider bandwidths like 200MHz.

title: Discrete-Time Value Iteration-Based Position Tracking Control for Electro-Hydraulic Systems With Unmeasured States
authors: Ashok Kumar Kumawat, Raja Rout, Pramila
abstract: novel infinite-horizon linear quadratic tracking (LQT) problem for the discrete-time system with unmeasured states is presented in this paper. The reference trajectory dynamics and the electro-hydraulic system are combined to create an augmented system. A new Bellman equation is derived to evaluate the value function associated with the devised policy. The LQT problem of the augmented system dynamics is solved by adaptive dynamic programming. Herein, a discrete-time value iteration (VI) algorithm is proposed that generates a control signal for the proportional directional control (PDC) valve, satisfying the actuation limits of [-10V, 10V]. To confirm the efficacy of the proposed control approach, three experiments utilizing square wave and multi-step reference trajectories are shown. The experimental findings verify that tracking error converges to an arbitrarily tiny neighborhood and that the electro-hydraulic actuator system’s tracking performance is enhanced.

title: Effects of the oxygen content of a-IGZO field-effect transistors on their density of states and dopant concentration lateral profile regarding oxygen scavenging
authors: SEong Hoon Jeon, Wonjung Kim, Seungki Kim, Seung Joo Myoung, Dong Hyeop Shin, Dong Myong Kim, Sung-Jin Choi, Yoon Jung Lee, Changwook Kim, Dae Hwan Kim
abstract: The oxygen flow rate (OFR) during sputtering deposition of amorphous indium–gallium–zinc–oxide (a-IGZO) was systematically adjusted to modulate the oxygen vacancy (VO) concentration in the channel, yielding O–poor, O–mid, and O–rich FETs. Under identical source/drain metal and annealing conditions, a pronounced oxygen scavenging (OS) effect was observed at lower OFRs, resulting in significant source/drain contact resistance (RSD) reduction due to the lowered VO formation energy. However, while reduced oxygen content effectively suppressed RSD, it simultaneously induced undesirable threshold voltage (VT) shifts caused by OS-driven carrier diffusion from the source/drain regions into the channel. Consequently, a clear trade-off between RSD reduction and VT stability was identified, wherein lower OFR enhanced RSD suppression at the expense of VT controllability. In contrast, higher OFR stabilized VT with limited RSD improvement. An optimal OFR was therefore proposed by decoupling the OS contributions to RSD and VT. In addition, the VT shift increased with decreasing channel length, and this tendency was more evident at lower OFRs.

title: FusionGuard-E2P: A Deviation-Guided and Imbalance-Aware Multimodal Framework for Insider Threat Detection
authors: Rubina Akter, Anik Islam, Hadis Karimipour, Fattane Zarrinkalam, Benjamin Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Insider threat detection (ITD) remains a significant challenge in organizational cybersecurity. This is due to subtle behavioral patterns, pronounced class imbalance, and the need to integrate heterogeneous data sources. Traditional methods, relying on audit logs and rule-based heuristics, often fail to capture latent behavioral intent and complex feature interactions. To address these limitations, this article introduces FusionGuard-E2P, a lightweight multimodal deep learning (DL) framework. It integrates textual embeddings with structured behavioral and psychometric features for robust ITD. The proposed architecture uses a convolutional feature interaction module to process fixed-length text embeddings. It combines these with tabular features via a multilayer perceptron (MLP), avoiding inappropriate sequential modeling of tabular data while effectively capturing nonlinear feature interactions. To further mitigate class imbalance, a deviation-aware generative adversarial network (GAN)-based augmentation strategy is introduced to enhance minority-class representation. Extensive experiments on the CERT 4.2 and CERT 5.2 datasets show that this framework achieves competitive performance in both binary and multiclass settings. It maintains strong generalization and computational efficiency. These findings highlight the effectiveness of combining multimodal feature learning with imbalance-aware optimization for practical ITD.

title: Strategizing At Speed: A Learned Model Predictive Game for Multi-Agent Drone Racing_supp1-3701568.mp4
authors: Andrei-Carlo Papuc
abstract: Autonomous drone racing pushes the boundaries of high-speed motion planning and multi-agent strategic decision-making. Success in this domain requires drones not only to navigate at their limits but also to anticipate and counteract competitors' actions. In this paper, we study a fundamental question that arises in this domain: how deeply should an agent strategize before taking an action? To this end, we compare two planning paradigms: the Model Predictive Game (MPG), which finds interaction-aware strategies at the expense of longer computation times, and contouring Model Predictive Control (MPC), which computes strategies rapidly but does not reason about interactions. We perform extensive experiments to study this trade-off, revealing that MPG outperforms MPC at moderate velocities but loses its advantage at higher speeds due to latency. To address this shortcoming, we propose a Learned Model Predictive Game (LMPG) approach that amortizes model predictive gameplay to reduce latency. In both simulation and hardware experiments, we benchmark our approach against MPG and MPC in head-to-head races, finding that LMPG outperforms both baselines.

title: Enhancing AI Powered Cyber Defense: An Explainable and Resilient Framework for Future Intelligent Warfare
authors: B.Ujalesh Subudhi, Bandhan Panda, Santosh Kumar Kar, Brojo Kishore Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Comparing the situation to the current dynamic digital environment, this paper identifies the novel contribution of the Artificial Intelligence to the improvement of the contemporary cyber security defense mechanism. The paper proposes an innovative AI enabled defense framework to overcome the pressures of the next-generation warfare and the problematic hybrid states. Unlike the conventional approaches that mainly focus on predictive threat recognition and automated recovery when it comes to the topic of cyber security of the enterprise, this research broadens the range of the AI implementation to the national security and tactical defense. The framework combines the notion of cyber resilience, explainable AI (XAI), and autonomous decision making intelligence in a synergistic way to make the operations more transparent, flexible, and strong. The system provides adaptive threat detection systems and dynamic response systems that are able to handle both known and a zero day attacks through the integration of deep learning and reinforcement learning. Special resilience layer will guarantee continuity in case of severe disruption, whereas XAI will advance interpretability and accountability so that defense officers can comprehend and rely on AI-based insights. The assessment outcomes have shown a high level of accuracy in response speed, accuracy in the analysis, and fault tolerance when compared to the current models based on AI. This study indicates that adaptive <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> architectures ethically managed can transform militant and cyber security defense, making AI one of the primary elements in the defense of resiliency, transparency, and readiness against future challenges.

title: Quantifying Narrative Dominance: The “Authenticity Trap” and Data-Driven Decision Support in Cognitive Warfare
authors: Nishank Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Present day conflicts around the globe have proved that it is not only the kinetic domain that matters for the decision matrix, but also several non-kinetic factors that steer much of pioneer decision making. While traditional intelligence frameworks remain glued to correctness of data and its authenticity, here we re-contest that this practice creates a critical void and deep overlooking vulnerability, which we coin as the Authenticity Trap. Non-kinetic Warfare, especially cognitive space warfare (CW), weaponizes these blind spots by populating social channels with fake, immaterial, and inauthentic contents that, while logically and technically unverifiable, drive genuine and often destabilizing behavioral changes in masses and targeted populations. Therefore, to address this void, we introduce the Social Domain Scrutiny Engine (SDSE) and a key metric called the Divergence Index (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D_{i}$</tex>). With our analysis of our dataset, which we name Operation Azure, and our reconstruction of recent geopolitical crises from 2019 to 2024, we demonstrate that filtering out adversarial noise results in a 48-to-72 hour latency in threat detection. Conversely, our SDSE model successfully predicts kinetic escalation by interpreting divergence <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D_{i}$</tex> not as noise, but as a critical information signal.

title: Faculty'S Role in the Artificial Intelligence Era. Our Continuing Changing Challenge
authors: Ana Luna, Agatha Clarice da Silva Ovando, Daniel Jurburg, Keyla Roman, Mario Chong
abstract: Society is changing at a remarkable pace, and Artificial Intelligence (AI) has rapidly become one of the most influential forces driving this transformation. Higher education is directly affected: while students tend to adopt AI tools with enthusiasm-drawn by their efficiency, accessibility, and appeal-faculty are still defining how these technologies fit into their teaching and professional identity. This study examines how university faculty perceive their role in the classroom in the context of AI. Using a qualitative survey design across multiple disciplines, it draws on the perspectives of 36 faculty members to explore evolving responsibilities, teaching methods, and student engagement. The results indicate that although AI is already widely adopted in teaching practice, its integration remains uneven, with significant concerns around plagiarism, weakened critical thinking, and insufficient institutional support. The findings underscore the importance of the faculty as a facilitator and strategic integrator of AI, ensuring that technology complements pedagogical judgment and that education keeps pace with innovation and its broader social impact.

title: Detection of Myocardial Infarction Using VCG and Neural Networks Based on Cardiac Wall Analysis
authors: Pedro Antônio A. Silva, João Neto Silva de Lima, Villeneve de Oliveira Soares, Emanuel Thiago A. Silva, Carlos Danilo M. Regis
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: This work proposes myocardial infarction classification using a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) network based on VCG signals, applying Reconstructed Phase Space (RPS) and segmentation into anterior (AMI) and inferior (IMI) walls. RPS-derived parameters and additional features (age, sex) were used as input. Wall-based segmentation and model combination achieved an accuracy of 77.57%, recall of 87.45%, and F1-Score of 0.7959, outperforming the non-segmented approach. Results demonstrate the clinical potential of this strategy for reliable infarction detection.

title: Integrating an Educational Chatbot Into Higher Education: A Case Study in Telecommunication Engineering
authors: David Mata-Moya, Nerea del-Rey-Maestre, Judith Redoli-Granados, Pilar Garcia-Diaz
abstract: This paper presents the design, implementation, and evaluation of a GPT-based educational chatbot developed with the Chatbase platform to enhance collaborative work and meaningful learning for students in the Radiation and Radiocommunication course of the Telecommunication Engineering degree at the University of Alcalá (Spain). The chatbot was trained with curated course materials, including lecture notes, laboratory manuals, textbooks, and ITU-R recommendations, and refined through iterative prompt-engineering techniques to improve response accuracy and relevance. Three pilot studies were carried out in real classroom settings involving undergraduate students, comparing the specialized chatbot with general-purpose tools such as ChatGPT and ChatPDF. Quantitative and qualitative data were collected through problem-solving tasks, accuracy analysis, and a perception survey. Results show that the proposed chatbot provided clearer and more targeted explanations, supported students' reasoning during laboratory tasks, and was perceived as a useful peer-like assistant that saved time and improved motivation. The study highlights the potential of tailored large-language-model-driven chatbots as complementary resources in engineering education, as well as the challenges regarding reasoning with numeric problems and the need for ethical guidelines in classroom adoption.

title: PM2.5 Forecasting in Almaty: A Benchmarking of Advanced Deep Learning and Hybrid Models
authors: Khaled Mohamad, Nuray Dauletkhan, Amani Ibraheem, Mohammed Alaa Ala’Anzy
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Accurate short-term forecasting of fine particulate matter (PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf>) is critical for public health protection in Almaty, Kazakhstan, where complex topography and meteorological conditions intensify pollution episodes. This study benchmarks statistical, tree-based, deep learning, and hybrid models for daily PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> prediction using 1,558 observations collected between February 2020 and May 2024. The evaluated methods include ARIMA, LightGBM with lag features, ARIMA+XGBoost, Wavelet+XGBoost, CNN–LSTM with wavelet smoothing, CNN– LSTM–Transformer, GRU, and a Seq2Seq GRU with attention. Missing values were handled using Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE), and model performance was assessed using MAE, RMSE, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> under a chronological train–validation split. GRU-based architectures achieved the highest accuracy (GRU: R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.8896, RMSE = 0.2768; Seq2Seq+Attention: R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.8889, RMSE = 0.2776), while wavelet preprocessing enhanced stability in convolutional and boosting models. The results highlight trade-offs between predictive accuracy, interpretability, and computational cost, and suggest future directions including multi-day forecasting, spatiotemporal extensions, explainability via SHAP and attention visualization, and real-time deployment.

title: Interpretable Two-Stage PPG Framework for Cerebrovascular Risk Assesment via Intermediate Blood Pressure Modeling
authors: Hossein Esmaeili, Hossein Marvi, Ali Kermani
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Cerebrovascular disorders are a major cause of death and long-term disability worldwide. Photoplethysmography (PPG), an optical modality that is already embedded in many consumer wearables, offers a low-cost, noninvasive window into vascular physiology. Rather than attempting end-to-end classification from large sets of handcrafted or learned PPG features, we hypothesize that chronic elevation of arterial blood pressure is the principal physiological mediator that links cerebrovascular pathology to observable changes in peripheral PPG waveforms. To test this hypothesis, we propose a transparent two-stage pipeline. In Stage I, short PPG segments are transformed into spectral-cepstral descriptors and used to estimate systolic and diastolic blood pressure via an ensemble of bagged regression trees. In Stage II, the mean estimated systolic and diastolic pressures for each subject serve as the sole inputs to a conventional classifier that predicts cerebrovascular risk (three classes: healthy, cerebral hypoperfusion, confirmed disease). On the public PPG-BP dataset, this minimal two-feature representation yielded 94.3% classification accuracy and AUC = 0.96 substantially outperforming typical single-stage models. Our findings emphasize the dominant role of blood pressure as a mediator of PPG-detectable cerebrovascular alterations and raise concerns about potential label leakage in common benchmarks. We discuss implications for model interpretability and for the design of robust wearable-based screening tools.

title: Development of a Secure IoT-Based System for Real-Time Monitoring of Medical Image Equipment
authors: Peyman Vafadoost Sabzevar, Alireza Chamansara, Ahmad Hajipour
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Considering the high cost of medical equipment—particularly imaging devices such as CT scanners, MRI machines, and ultrasound systems—and the challenges associated with their maintenance and spare parts supply, ensuring the protection and continuous monitoring of such devices is of critical importance. Excessive use of these devices, especially during health crises such as pandemics, significantly increases the risk of wear and damage. Therefore, smart monitoring of the environmental conditions of medical equipment, along with timely alerts to authorized users when predefined thresholds are exceeded, plays a vital role in extending device lifespan and reducing maintenance costs. Moreover, since the transmission of sensitive data from clients to a central server is essential in this context, securing communications and detecting potential cyberattacks become critical concerns. This paper presents the development of a secure IoT-based hardware and software platform for monitoring the environmental parameters of medical equipment. The proposed system not only enhances the management and operational efficiency of these devices but also strengthens the overall cybersecurity of the monitoring infrastructure.

title: A Novel Approach to Silicon Pitting Mitigation - The Role of Oxygen in Annealing Processes
authors: Adukkadukkam Dineshan, Chen Yan Connie, Wong Michelle Yuan Yuan, Islam Mohd Nurul
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Silicon pitting defects pose a critical challenge to yield and device reliability in advanced CMOS manufacturing. This work identifies a previously unreported mechanism linking pitting formation to oxygen-deficient conditions during high-temperature furnace annealing. Systematic DOEs—including furnace position studies, tool-to-tool comparisons, and tests using both patterned and un-patterned wafers—demonstrate that wafers exposed to even trace oxygen during the initial minutes of a 1150 °C anneal remain free of defects, whereas shielded wafers consistently exhibit severe pitting. The findings show that insufficient oxygen promotes volatile SiO formation, which destabilizes the thin SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> surface layer, exposes underlying silicon, and generates localized stress and dislocation networks. These weakened regions subsequently undergo selective removal during STI nitride strip, manifesting as pits. Introducing a brief, low-flow O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> pulse at the start of annealing completely suppresses the defect without affecting oxide thickness. GOF scatterometry is further shown to be a sensitive, non-destructive indicator of surface instability.

title: Comparative Analysis under Laboratory Conditions of the Neutral Grounding Techniques in Electrical Networks
authors: Daniela-Florentina Roșca, Ana-Bianca Mititelu, Pavel Atănăsoae
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Neutral point treatment is a fundamental aspect of the design and operation of electrical power generation, transmission, distribution, and consumption networks. This paper presents an analysis of neutral point treatment techniques used in medium-voltage electrical distribution networks, with a focus on system behavior in the event of single-phase faults. The study comparatively analyzes, under laboratory conditions, two competing neutral treatment methods that result in increased currents and voltages, respectively, in the event of a single-phase fault: the isolated neutral and the grounded neutral. To investigate the system’s behavior, a simulation model of a medium-voltage distribution network was developed in the MATLAB Simulink environment. The phenomena that occur during short circuits and ground faults are highlighted and validated using the laboratory test bench.

title: Language Identification in Multilingual Texts using a Shared Alphabet via Fractal Dimension Analysis
authors: Zeinab Khodabakhshi, Ali Valizadeh, Hamid Hassanpour
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Languages exhibit various complexities across grammatical, lexical, phonological, and orthographic dimensions. This research addresses the challenge of identifying multiple languages in texts, especially when different languages share the same alphabet, which limits the effectiveness of traditional lexical and orthographic methods. Existing approaches often depend on extensive linguistic resources or complex feature extraction, resulting in high computational demands and reduced performance on short-text segments. To overcome these limitations, a method employing fractal dimension analysis is proposed. In this approach, the text is treated as time series via replacing characters in the text by their associated ASCII codes. The Higuchi fractal algorithm is applied on the time series within a sliding window to quantify the structural complexity of these sequences. The resulting signals are smoothed using a moving average, and their statistical measures—mean and standard deviation are employed to detect points where language switching occur within the text. Evaluation on a multilingual English text containing seven languages—Persian, Arabic, Chinese, German, Italian, French, and English—demonstrates an accuracy of 91.5% in correctly identifying language switching. The approach is computationally efficient and capable of identification language switch within texts shorter than a sentence. The proposed method results demonstrate that measures of structural complexity have potential utility for language identification in single sentence or even shorter than a sentence, owing to their resource efficiency.

title: ViM-Q: Scalable Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Vision Mamba Model Inference on FPGA
authors: Shengzhe Lyu, Yuhan She, Patrick S. Y. Hung, Ray C. C. Cheung, Weitao Xu
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Vision Mamba (ViM) models offer a compelling efficiency advantage over Transformers by leveraging the linear complexity of State Space Models (SSMs), yet efficiently deploying them on FPGAs remains challenging. Linear layers struggle with dynamic activation outliers that render static quantization ineffective, while uniform quantization fails to capture the weight distribution at low bit-widths. Furthermore, while associative scan accelerates SSMs on GPUs, its memory access patterns are misaligned with the streaming dataflow required by FPGAs. To address these challenges, we present ViM-Q <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>, a scalable algorithm-hardware co-design for end-to-end ViM inference on the edge. We introduce a hardware-aware quantization scheme combining dynamic per-token activation quantization and per-channel smoothing to mitigate outliers, alongside a custom 4-bit per-block Additive Power-of-Two (APoT) weight quantization. The models are deployed on a runtime-parameterizable FPGA accelerator featuring a linear engine employing a Lookup-Table (LUT) unit to replace multiplications with shift-add operations, and a fine-grained pipelined SSM engine that parallelizes the state dimension while preserving sequential recurrence. Crucially, the hardware supports runtime configuration, adapting to diverse dimensions and input resolutions across the ViM family. Implemented on an AMD ZCU102 FPGA, ViM-Q achieves an average 4.96× speedup and 59.8× energy efficiency gain over a quantized NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU baseline for low-batch inference on ViM-tiny. This co-design shows a viable path for deploying ViM models on resource-constrained edge devices.

title: Comparisons of Electric Field Profiles in Various Oil-Paper Insulation Structures of Power Transformers
authors: Sicheng Zhao, Zhiheng Li, Junchao Feng, Wu Lu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Offshore wind power transformers are critical components of wind farm systems, and the performance of their oil-paper insulation systems directly determines the operational reliability and service life of the transformers. Therefore, research on oil-paper insulation systems under various operating conditions is of great significance for ensuring the long-term safe operation of the equipment. This paper focuses on the oil-paper insulation system commonly found in oil-filled equipment within new-type power systems. By investigating the influence of the oil-paper structure and the permittivity of the insulation paper on streamer development, the study aims to optimize the oil-paper insulation system. A model of the oil-paper insulation system was established using COMSOL Multiphysics simulation software to examine streamer variations under different conditions. The research indicates that in a vertical insulation paper configuration, a larger difference in relative permittivity between the oil and the paper causes the streamer to propagate more along the oil-paper interface, with a faster propagation speed and a thinner streamer channel. Conversely, a smaller difference results in a thicker streamer, a slower propagation speed, and propagation away from the interface within the oil gap. In a parallel configuration, a higher permittivity of the oil-paper causes the streamer to rapidly reach the insulation paper interface, forming a relatively stable surface flashover. When the permittivity difference is smaller, the streamer flows downward with an increasingly smaller column diameter.

title: Diagnosis Method for Operating Conditions of Substation Equipment based on Voiceprint Recognition Technology
authors: Shujian Huang, Shihao Yang, Jingtang Liang, Huanshen Zhang, Yongxiang Xie, Jianrong Liu, Yubin Ren, Haolin Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The operation status of substation equipment directly affects the economic production of society and people's daily lives, so the monitoring of the working conditions of substation equipment is an important issue. To ensure the safe and stable operation of the substation, it is also important to determine the working condition of the equipment by listening to the sound of the substation equipment. This article first uses multiple microphones to obtain the sound of equipment operating conditions and environmental sounds, providing a complete audio record for audio analysis to obtain pure audio information of the equipment. On the other hand, a three-dimensional vibration sensor is used to obtain the vibration signal of the substation equipment, which complements the audio signal to form a complete signal record of the equipment operating conditions; Secondly, using Kalman filtering to improve the anti-interference ability of the acquisition device; Finally, the improved support vector machine algorithm is combined to accurately classify the processed sound signals. Greatly improve the operational reliability of equipment and the work efficiency of personnel, reducing the occurrence of accidents and incidents.

title: Distributed Adaptive Robust Voltage/VAR Control for Active Distribution Networks
authors: Yanyan Liang, Jiaxiao Wang, Chengcheng Wu, Peng Xu, Can Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a Distributed Adaptive Robust Voltage/VAR Control (DAR-VVC) method for active distribution networks to handle voltage deviations and operational uncertainty caused by fluctuations of distributed renewable generation and load demand, aiming to ensure safe operation while robustly minimizing network power loss under uncertainty. A partition-based distributed framework is adopted. Considering the different dispatch time scales of OLTCs, capacitor banks (CBs), and PV inverters, the VVC operates in a two-stage manner: OLTCs and CBs are scheduled hourly in the first stage, while PV-inverter reactive power setpoints are updated every 15 minutes in the second stage. The problem is formulated as a mixed-integer second-order cone program (MISOCP) minimizing power losses, where boundary-consistency constraints characterize inter-partition coupling to enable distributed coordination. For network partitioning, an electrical distance defined by the sensitivity between voltage magnitude and reactive-power injection is used to build the similarity matrix for APCA-based self-learning clustering, and the Davies–Bouldin index (DBI) is employed to determine a proper number of subnetworks. Uncertainty is addressed via a "min – max – min" adaptive robust optimization (ARO) formulation with an uncertainty budget to balance robustness and conservativeness.

title: TrojanGPL: Vulnerability Analysis and Mitigation of Backdoor Attacks on Graph Prompt Learning
authors: Xiaoting Lyu, Yufei Han, Hangwei Qian, Ivor W. Tsang, Wei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Graph Prompt Learning (GPL) effectively bridges the gap between graph pre-training and downstream tasks, addressing the bottleneck in knowledge transfer. However, its susceptibility to backdoor attacks during pre-training remains largely unexplored. This work systematically investigates the backdoor risks in GPL and introduces TrojanGPL-a cross-context backdoor attack that embeds triggers solely through manipulations of the pre-trained encoder, independent of downstream knowledge. Our analysis reveals that jointly optimizing trigger graphs with the prompt mechanism not only narrows the gap between upstream and downstream main tasks but also aligns main and backdoor learning objectives, facilitating seamless backdoor transfer from pre-trained models to diverse downstream applications. To counter this threat, we propose ES-Detector, a defense mechanism that identifies backdoored graph samples by detecting discriminative embedding shifts upon the removal of important nodes. Specifically, when clean and backdoored graphs are predicted as the same class, clean graphs exhibit stable embedding changes, whereas backdoored graphs show significant deviations from clean ones. Extensive experiments on 5 benchmark datasets and 3 GPL frameworks demonstrate that TrojanGPL achieves an attack success rate exceeding 90% in different cross-context scenarios while minimally impacting the performance of the main task. Meanwhile, ES-Detector reduces the attack success rate by at least 60% across all the test settings, validating its practical robustness without prior knowledge of attack patterns.

title: Lightweight Multi-Modal Transformer for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting with Sky Imagery and Meteorological Data
authors: Peng Gao, Ying Li, Wenzheng Song
abstract: The increasing penetration of photovoltaic generation systems into modern power grids necessitates accurate short-term power forecasting to ensure reliable grid operations and optimal energy management. Traditional forecasting approaches relying solely on meteorological measurements often fail to capture rapid atmospheric changes that critically affect solar irradiance. This paper proposes a lightweight multi-modal transformer architecture that integrates sky imagery with meteorological time-series data through cross-attention fusion mechanisms for enhanced photovoltaic power forecasting. The framework employs parallel convolutional and transformer encoders to extract complementary spatial and temporal features from sky images and weather measurements, enabling the model to capture both gradual meteorological trends and abrupt cloud-induced irradiance fluctuations. Experimental validation on a real-world dataset, averaged over five independent random seeds, demonstrates that the proposed approach achieves a normalized mean absolute error of 0.0999 ± 0.0037 and a weighted mean absolute percentage error of 11.81 ± 0.44%, corresponding to a 16.4% improvement over the best unimodal baseline. The cross-attention mechanism enables weather-adaptive visual feature selection, with recurring high-response bands on the visual time axis indicating that the model concentrates on a limited subset of historical sky observations rather than weighting all visual tokens uniformly. The lightweight architecture with only 32×32 pixel image resolution achieves superior performance compared to higher resolutions while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for operational deployment in photovoltaic forecasting systems.

title: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems Information for Authors
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems

title: Quantum Machine Learning for IoT: Trends, Challenges, and Technology Readiness
authors: Chirag Sharma, Kashvi Sood, Sandeep Kumar Sood
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) merges Quantum Computing (QC) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) to solve problems that are intractable by classical computers. At the same time, domains such as finance, healthcare, manufacturing, and transportation domains are transitioning into Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled cyber physical systems generating streams of heterogeneous data with high dimensionality and volume. This work presents a unified scientometric and qualitative review of QML across these sectors. A PRISMA-based screening is followed by a Keyword Co-occurrence Cluster Analysis (KCCA), a Keyword Burst Analysis (KBA), and a Citation Burst Analysis (CBA) using CiteSpace on a Scopus-derived corpus of literature. An evidence-based Technology Readiness Level (TRL) framework is introduced to assess the technology’s maturity. Our findings indicate that the volume of publications has increased after 2022, with healthcare dominating the volume and manufacturing the impact. Furthermore, a clear trend towards the development of hybrid quantum classical architectures is present across all domains. Security applications such as anomaly detection are the most developed QML entry point to IoT systems. All four domains are at a TRL of 3-4, indicating that QML research is still at the concept-validation stage. Challenges include hardware noise, barren plateaus, absence of standardized benchmarks, and encoding heterogeneous data from IoT devices for training QML applications.

title: Improving Convergence for Semi-Federated Learning: An Energy-Efficient Approach by Manipulating Over-the-Air Distortion
authors: Jingheng Zheng, Hui Tian, Wanli Ni, Yang Tian, Ping Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we propose a hybrid learning framework that combines federated and split learning, termed semi-federated learning (SemiFL), in which over-the-air computation is utilized for gradient aggregation. A key idea is to strategically adjust the learning rate by manipulating over-the-air distortion for improving SemiFL’s convergence. Specifically, we intentionally amplify amplitude distortion to increase the learning rate in the non-stable region, thereby accelerating convergence and reducing communication energy consumption. In the stable region, we suppress noise perturbation to maintain a small learning rate for improving SemiFL’s final convergence. Theoretical results demonstrate the antagonistic effects of over-the-air distortion in different regions, under both independent and identically distributed (IID) and non-IID data settings. Then, we formulate two energy consumption minimization problems, one for each region, which implements a two-region mean square error threshold configuration scheme. Accordingly, we propose two resource allocation algorithms with closed-form solutions. Simulation results show that under different network and data distribution conditions, strategically manipulating over-the-air distortion can efficiently adjust the learning rate to improve SemiFL’s convergence. Moreover, energy consumption can be reduced by using the proposed algorithms.

title: FreqMamba: Frequency Enhancement and Mamba-Driven Representation Learning for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
authors: Junyi Wu, Yan Huang, Min Gao, Yuzhen Niu, Fusheng Li, Yuzhong Chen, Zhang Zhang, Qiang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: This paper focuses on research into multimodal pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR). Existing multimodal PAR methods face three major challenges: (1) reliance on fixed prompt templates that limit refined textual attribute descriptions, (2) neglect of essential visual details, such as textures and contours, which are crucial for distinguishing subtle attributes, and (3) insufficient interaction between visual and textual modalities, resulting in suboptimal recognition performance. To address these challenges, we propose a Frequency Enhancement and Mamba-Driven Representation Learning (FreqMamba) framework. Specifically, we design a Dynamic Prompt Strategy (DPS) that dynamically adapts textual descriptions based on visual features, resulting in refined textual descriptions. Additionally, a Frequency-Enhanced Module (FEM) is introduced to capture texture and contour details in the frequency domain, enriching visual features and further enhancing the generation of dynamic prompts. Finally, we propose a Mamba-Driven Modality Interaction (MDMI) method combined with a Bidirectional Modality Contrastive Loss (BMCLoss) to facilitate effective interaction between modalities, integrating visual and textual information comprehensively. Extensive experiments on multiple PAR datasets, including PETA, PA100K, RAPv1, and RAPv2, demonstrate the superiority of our FreqMamba approach over existing methods.

title: A guided residual search for nonlinear state-space identification
authors: Merijn Floren, Jan Swevers
abstract: Identifying the parameters of nonlinear state-space models from input-output data typically requires solving a highly non-convex optimization problem, which is prone to slow convergence and suboptimal local solutions. This work improves the reliability and efficiency of the estimation process by decomposing the overall optimization problem into a sequence of tractable subproblems. Starting from a linear baseline model, nonlinear residual dynamics are first estimated using a guided residual search (GRS) and subsequently refined through multiple-shooting optimization. Experiments on two benchmarks show competitive performance with state-of-the-art black-box methods and improved convergence over naive initialization.

title: Martian Mineral Formation Analysis Based on Martian Mineral Corpus and Knowledge Graph
authors: Zhe Wang, Tian Ke, Weihao Shen, Yinhe Liu, Yanfei Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Martian minerals are key indicators of the planet’s geological evolution and potential habitability. Their formation mechanisms record critical evidence of early aqueous activity, surface geochemistry, and geological activities. However, analyzing these mechanisms requires integrating heterogeneous data from spectroscopy, geomorphology, and stratigraphy. Existing studies still rely heavily on manual interpretation of heterogeneous data, resulting in inconsistent conclusions and a time-consuming reasoning process. To address this challenge, we build the first unified Martian Mineral Corpus and Martian Mineral Knowledge Graph (MMKG). The corpus integrates global Mars data products and scientific literature, while the MMKG unifies mineral-related concepts with their semantic and spatiotemporal relations, providing comprehensive data and knowledge support for reasoning. On this basis, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is proposed as an intelligent question answering (QA) framework designed for Martian mineral analysis. RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> performs knowledge graph path retrieval under dual constraints: geological vectors distilled from the unified corpus guide multi-hop exploration, and geological knowledge-consistency validates candidate chains. This yields graph paths that are both context-aligned and causally plausible. Leveraging these paths as the backbone, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> integrates geological context from the corpus and the knowledge graph to perform unified reasoning. Experimental results show that RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> outperforms existing methods on Martian mineral question answering. For mineral formation mechanisms, RBG<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> covers over 90% of key points of experts’ analysis on average and produces reasoning that is both interpretable and traceable, achieving the first reliable and intelligent Martian mineral analysis. Code has been made available at: https://github.com/guoluqingfeng10086/MMQA.

title: The Investigation of Emotion Cognition in Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Multi-dimensional and Multi-stage Path Analysis_supp1-3701793.pdf
authors: Jingying Chen
abstract: Emotion cognition deficits are a core component of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and remain difficult to capture due to their dynamic and cross-stage nature. This study proposes a multimodal stage-aware structural equation modeling (MS-ASEM) framework for interpretable emotion cognition assessment in ASD by integrating overt behavioral responses and covert physiological states. Specifically, this framework employs synchronized eye-tracking (ET), electroencephalography (EEG), electrodermal activity (EDA), and facial expression (FE) data to quantify four stages of emotional processing: perception, understanding, arousal, and expression. A structural equation model is developed to uncover cross-stage dependencies and atypical pathways in ASD. Experimental results with 40 children diagnosed with ASD and 50 typically developing children demonstrate a unique “high-arousal, low-integration” profile in ASD, characterized by hypersensitivity to negative stimuli, atypical prefrontal activation, and desynchronized multimodal responses. The proposed path model further reveals impaired transmission between perception and understanding, as well as inhibitory coupling between arousal and expression. Overall, the framework effectively bridges computational assessment with structural interpretability, highlighting the advantages of stageaware interpretable multimodal modeling in mechanism-oriented affective computing research.

title: A Hybrid Model for Short-Term Wind Power Prediction Incorporating Improved CEEMDAN and Causal Feature Selection
authors: Yiming Mao, Yanying Li, Shunping Wang, Yongjie Li, Siyu Zhang, Zirong Li, Lele Jiang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the limitations of traditional wind power forecasting methods in effectively handling data non-stationarity and complex causal relationships, this study proposes a novel ultra-short-term wind power forecasting model that integrates causal inference and deep learning. First, the model employs an improved CEEMDAN algorithm to adaptively decompose the original power sequence, extracting clear temporal modes and mitigating the mode mixing effect. Subsequently, a Causal Forest is utilized for feature screening to identify external proxy variables that exhibit strong predictive value and statistical correlation with power fluctuations, thereby enhancing model interpretability without making excessive physical causal assumptions. Building upon this, a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) is constructed as the core predictor, with a Bayesian optimization method introduced to automatically search for its optimal hyperparameters. Finally, a Random Forest algorithm is applied to correct the prediction residuals, further improving the output accuracy. A case study conducted on a large-scale wind farm in Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, China, demonstrates that the proposed model achieves an MSE of 0.1100, an RMSE of 0.3316, an MAE of 0.1889, and an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.8956 on the test set, significantly outperforming other benchmark models. The proposed framework not only effectively processes the non-stationarity and complexity of wind power data but also enhances interpretability through prudent statistical feature selection, providing more reliable forecasting support for wind farm operation management and power grid dispatching.

title: A Distribution Network Reconfiguration Method Considering Flexible Regulation of Air-Conditioning Loads
authors: Ting Yang, Yuxuan Ren, Kaiwen Jiang, Jingjing Bai
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the advancement of carbon-neutrality targets and faster urbanization, the share of air-conditioning (AC) loads in distribution systems continues to increase. These loads, with their seasonal and concentrated characteristics, can easily lead to feeder overloads and even outages. Conventional reconfiguration models usually treat loads as rigid demands. As a result, they fail to exploit the potential of AC loads as large-scale, adjustable flexibility resources. To address this gap, this paper proposes a topology-optimized distribution network reconfiguration method that explicitly considers the flexible regulation characteristics of AC loads. For typical scenarios such as conventional economic operation, a reconfiguration model is formulated with network loss minimization as the objective, while incorporating the shiftability of AC loads. Case studies on a modified IEEE 33-bus system under typical scenarios show that, under the economic operation scenario, with AC loads actively participating in regulation, the proposed method can effectively reduce network losses compared with conventional strategies. The results provide a practical technical route for building a new reconfiguration framework for modern distribution systems.

title: Impedance Stability Analysis and Enhancement Strategies for Photovoltaic-Storage Microgrids
authors: Zhengtong Yang, Jinyu Wang, Wenxin Yi, Lei Chen, Haoran Wang, Bobo Zhang, Zhijie Li, Jiapeng Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the rapid development of distributed power sources and energy storage integration, the power supply reliability of distribution networks has become a prominent issue. It is urgent to fully utilize the adjustable resources and controllable equipment of the active distribution network to improve the power supply reliability of the distribution network. Distribution substations powered by power electronic converters face severe instability risks due to their low inertia, weak damping characteristics, and lack of frequency and voltage support from large power grids. This paper first establishes an impedance model for distribution substations operating independently when disconnected from the grid, and analyzes the impact of system parameters on system stability based on the established model. Then, based on the analysis results, a method to enhance stability by reshaping the converter impedance characteristics is proposed. Finally, the analysis results and improvement strategy are verified through simulations in MATLAB/SIMULINK.

title: Machine Unlearning in Smart Grids: A Cautionary Tale of Infrastructure Vulnerability_supp1-3701875.pdf
authors: George Paphitis

title: Accurate Temporal Prior Modeling for Learned Video Compression
authors: Dongjian Yang, Xiaopeng Fan, Debin Zhao
abstract: Recently, learned video compression (LVC) has achieved remarkable progress. Accurate temporal prior modeling is crucial for the rate-distortion (RD) performance of LVC. However, existing methods often suffer from imprecise temporal priors due to limited reconstruction ability of frame decoders and insufficient exploitation of temporal information from reconstructed frames and features. To address these issues, we first design an enhanced feature reconstructor (EFR) by integrating content-aware depthwise separable convolution (CADSC) that excels at local modeling with efficient linear attention duality (ELAD) that facilitates complete global modeling. Based on the EFR, an enhanced frame decoder (EFD) is constructed to improve transform capability, thereby generating high-quality reconstructed frames and features. Furthermore, to fully mine temporal information from these high-quality reconstructions, we propose a multi-type temporal prior mixer (MTPM). Specifically, the MTPM fuses short-term temporal information obtained via optical-flow-based feature warping with long-term temporal information captured by a proposed state-update-based method, thereby further improving the accuracy of temporal prior modeling. The temporal prior produced by the MTPM not only provides high-quality conditional guidance for frame encoding and decoding, but is also combined with the hyper prior, spatial prior, and the discrete latent representation of the previous frame to construct a diverse prior fusion entropy model (DPFEM), which further enhances entropy estimation accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing LVC models and achieves an average performance gain of 29.64% compared with the LDB configuration of the H.266/VVC reference software VTM20.2.

title: Dynamic scene graph for Multi-stage Prediction and Planning in Autonomous Driving
authors: Nan Xu, Chaoyi Chen, Liang Chu, Minghe Na, Chong Guo, Yifan Jia
abstract: Motion prediction and motion planning are critical modules in autonomous driving, and they exhibit a high degree of correlation. Many prior works treat prediction and planning as separate modules, which may cause inter-module error accumulation and make it difficult to capture evolving future interactions between the ego vehicle and surrounding traffic agents, especially in long-horizon interactive scenarios. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated prediction-planning framework, DSGPlanner, which iteratively performs prediction and planning, and constructs a dynamic scene graph from prediction results to explicitly model future interactions between the ego vehicle and other agents. Unlike single-stage interaction methods, this framework updates the scene graph at each time step, allowing interaction information to be progressively refined as predictions evolve, thereby effectively mitigating the loss of interaction cues in long-horizon planning. Extensive experiments on the nuPlan benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Furthermore, although integration can reduce information loss, it may exacerbate causal confusion. To alleviate causal confusion in the integrated prediction-planning model and enhance computational efficiency, a dynamic mask self-distillation method is also introduced.

title: An Analytical Theory of Avalanche Breakdown in p-n Junctions Derived From Semiconductor Band Structure
authors: Huayou Liu, Jing Zhang, Abdelmadjid Mesli, Yaping Dan
abstract: This article presents a comprehensive analytical theory for the avalanche breakdown process in p-n junction diodes to address the critical reliance on empirical models in the design of avalanche photodiodes (APDs) and single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs). The proposed model is derived from fundamental carrier continuity equations and impact excitation probability. Eventually, it yields closed-form expressions for the avalanche current density and the breakdown voltage. It demonstrates excellent agreement with experimental current–voltage characteristics across various semiconductors (Si, GaN, 4H-SiC, and InP) and a wide temperature range. TCAD simulations are also adopted to validate this model. The theory establishes a unified, physics-based criterion for defining the avalanche breakdown voltage, revealing its functional dependence on material parameters (bandgap, dielectric constant, and mean-free path) and doping concentrations. Significantly, it explicitly predicts the theoretical minimum and maximum limits for the breakdown voltage in a given material, which were well known but only implicitly reflected in previous empirical models. This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for the steady-state dark avalanche current for the targeted design of diverse avalanche-based devices, ranging from low-breakdown-voltage SPADs for integrated quantum photonics to high-breakdown-voltage power devices, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches.

title: Low-Frequency Magnetic Induction Tomography for Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles based Stroke Imaging_supp1-3699665.pdf
authors: Jian Sun
abstract: Stroke is a leading cause of mortality and disability worldwide, with rapid diagnosis critical for effective intervention. Conventional imaging modalities such as CT and MRI are limited by their size, cost, and unsuitability for bedside or prehospital emergency use. This study presents a novel Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles (SPIOs) based Magnetic Induction Tomography (SPIO-MIT) for non-invasive stroke-tracing imaging. Operating at an ultra-low excitation frequency of 3 kHz and a magnetic field strength below 1 mT, the system effectively suppresses background interference from biological tissues while enhancing sensitivity to SPIOs distribution. System performance was validated through phantom experiments featuring varied SPIOs positions, geometries, and concentration gradients, confirming accurate imaging of SPIOs with negligible response to low-conductivity substances. Complementary in vitro experiments embedding SPIO-blood mixtures in biological tissues demonstrated minimal background interference. In vivo experiments on a rabbit intracerebral hemorrhage model further confirmed precise localization of SPIOs injected into the brain, with detection sensitivity down to 1.2 mg of iron and an imaging diameter of 60 mm. In summary, the developed SPIO-MIT system enables sensitive, non-invasive spatial mapping of SPIOs. By combining the high sensitivity of magnetic particle imaging with the operational simplicity and biological safety of magnetic induction tomography, SPIO-MIT represents a promising technique for portable, bedside, and prehospital stroke diagnostics.

title: Adaptive Robust Predictive Direct Power Control With NN Compensation for 3L-AFE Rectifiers
authors: Zeyu Zhang, Jien Ma, Xing Liu, Chenghao Liu, Lin Qiu, Youtong Fang
abstract: Model predictive direct power control for the active front-end rectifier faces degraded control performance under parameter mismatches. To improve robustness to model uncertainties, this article proposes an adaptive robust predictive direct power control method. Specifically, the proposed design integrates the fitting capability of a super-twisting observer for unknown nonlinear dynamics to estimate aggregate disturbances, and simultaneously proposes a fast gradient method operating within the orthogonal complement of the state vector to update the input gain in real time, achieving the overall effect of the control process operating with adaptive capability and robustness against unknown system variations and model uncertainties. Furthermore, the developed solution takes actuator output deviations into account and uses a two-layer neural network for approximation, which corrects the real reference input. Moreover, by constructing a Lyapunov function, it is proved that the proposed control scheme ensures that the system error remains uniformly ultimately bounded. Finally, simulation and experimental results validate the findings.

title: Analytical Solution for Crosswell Electromagnetic Detection with Cased Boreholes
authors: Kai Zhao, Decheng Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: We propose an analytical algorithm for accurately solving the electromagnetic (EM) fields within a configuration of two parallel, multilayered cylindrical structures. In this model, a triaxial dipole transmitter is located in the innermost layer of the first cylinder, while EM multi-component measurements are recorded within the innermost layer of the second cylinder. The field is represented as a combination of outgoing waves and standing waves, with the use of the generalized reflection/transmission (R/T) method to characterize wave propagation. The core of the algorithm lies in the transformation between these wave types, achieved through the application of Graf's addition theorems across dual cylindrical coordinate systems. This approach enables the derivation of a super reflection coefficient on a fictitious boundary, specifically addressing eccentricity. By integrating this with the conventional generalized R/T method, the exact field at any spatial point can be determined. The derived formulae have been rigorously validated through numerical experiments and are applicable to crosswell EM investigations involving either single or double-cased wells.

title: Multi Gas Source Localisation through Candidate Sampling and Gated Cluster Selection
authors: Muhammad Talha Yasin, Rafiqul Islam, Matt Garratt
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Control, Automation and Robotics (ICCAR)
abstract: Localising multiple gas sources from sparse measurements remains a fundamental challenge in turbulent flow environments where plumes exhibit intermittent behavior and spatial overlap. This paper presents a two-stage probabilistic framework that addresses false-positive suppression through candidate-guided particle filtering and interpretable cluster gating. A Boids-based swarm executes structured sampling across a coarse grid to identify high-concentration candidate cells, focusing subsequent measurements on regions with maximum information content. A Particle Filter (PF) performs Bayesian inference exclusively on in-cell measurements, while an adaptive Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DB-SCAN) extracts source hypotheses from the weighted posterior. Four interpretable gating criteria—minimum particle count, local weight fraction, spatial spread, and inter-lock separation—reject transient clusters arising from plume intermittency. The complete pipeline is validated through extensive simulation using PomPy puff-plume dynamics and real-world arena trials with VICON-tracked Sphero RVRs. Experimental evaluation across varying wind directions, source counts, and challenging spatial configurations demonstrates detection rates exceeding 85% with substantially reduced false positives compared to ungated baselines, while maintaining sub-meter localisation accuracy and real-time execution on resource-constrained platforms. The framework operates without wind-direction estimation and scales linearly with source count, establishing practical feasibility for multi-source gas detection on low-cost mobile robots.

title: Blueprint Based Campus Navigation System with QR Scanning
authors: Chiranjeevi Aggala, B. Raghavaiah, M.L.S.N.S Lakshmi, G. Bindu Pavani, D. Hema Latha, K. Ramya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The proposed campus navigation system assists in locating buildings within the campus like canteens, libraries, hostels, playground and departmental buildings. It uses a pop-up map that uses X-Y coordinate system to guide the routes accurately. It is created using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and QR code technology and it allows to save time navigating the campus and be particularly useful during events, admission seasons, and orientation programs. This innovative and expandable product upgrades campus navigation and makes it hassle-free and simple to all users.

title: Electromagnetic Noise Analysis and Research on 10kV Medium-Voltage 36-Pole Circulating Pump Motor for a Nuclear Power Project
authors: Wenlei Zhang, Deyang Feng, Bin Xie
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: The circulating water pumps of Units 5 and 6 in a domestic nuclear power plant adopt the scheme of direct drive by metal mixed-flow pumps and motors. According to the process calculation requirements, the project plans to use 10kV medium-voltage 36-pole vertical asynchronous motors. The noise level of a motor is an important index to evaluate its quality, and how to effectively reduce motor noise is a key issue in motor design. This paper deeply analyzes the main sources and formation mechanism of the electromagnetic noise of this motor. In view of the causes of electromagnetic noise, specific noise reduction suggestions and control measures are proposed, which can significantly reduce the motor noise level and provide strong support for the stable operation of the circulating pump transmission system and environmental protection.

title: Natural Language Processing Techniques for Analysing Consumer Sentiments Toward Time-Honoured Brand Innovations
authors: Na Wang, Lusi Li
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: Text Classification, Opinion Mining, and Decision Making have become a requirement for businesses and researchers to understand what's hidden behind the lenses in the textual data set. Hence, Sentiment Analysis has taken forward its reach and importance as an important tool to classify the textual data, effectively extracting meaningful information from it. With this study, the traditional machine learning (ML) models, such as Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest are evaluated alongside with the deep learning (DL) models, such as Long ShortTerm Memory, Convolutional Neural Networks and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. According to the results observe that in IMDB and Yelp reviews, traditional ML models were moderately successful and the best model was SVM at 83.1 % and 80.5 % respectively. Despite that, deep learning models performed much better, managing to surpass ML approaches by a large margin: BERT was able to achieve 91.2 on IMDB and 89.1 on Yelp (on the latter two, both theoretically and in practice). Finally, our best results were obtained from the BERT + LSTM hybrid model, which had 0.938 degree of accuracy on IMDB, 0.914 on Yelp, 0.881 on Financial News and 0.863 on medical reviews, thus it enhanced the sentiment classification via contextual embeddings and sequential learning. It is demonstrated in the study that hybrid models improve sentiment classification accuracy under each domain. Future work will be on computational efficiency, application of multilingual adaptation, and domain specific tuning for best performance on business, finance, healthcare applications.

title: Granger-Inspired Predictive Structure Test for RBG/RNG Evaluation
authors: Joshua H. Sylvester, Micah A. Thornton, Mitchell A. Thornton, Eric C. Larson
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: New standards for post-quantum cryptography rely on new methods to evaluate the quality of random bit generators. Existing approaches largely rely on correlation-based tests, which are limited in their ability to capture dependence. In particular, they do not directly assess independence or reveal potential cause-and-effect relationships within the sequence. To address this gap, we introduce a methodology for evaluating random generators that draws on the concept of causality as a measure of predictability. We introduce the Granger-inspired Predictive Structure Test, which evaluates subsequences of a bitstream by comparing a logistic regression model against a null model, enabling the detection of predictive dependencies that correlationbased methods overlook. We show that, under assumptions aligning our method with a Granger causality test, the method detects long-term predictive dependencies and identifies nonrandomness at a significantly higher rate than widely used statistical tests.

title: Intelligent Feedback Analytics: Hybrid LSTM-GPT Framework for Educational Improvement
authors: Sali Radha Pranav, Sonali N. Jadhav, S. V. Gumaste, Sahil Patil
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: In today’s educational landscape, institutions are recognizing the importance of student feedback in improving teaching methods and learning experiences. However, traditional analysis methods, like manual reviews, surveys, and basic statistical tools, often fail to reveal the complex patterns within qualitative feedback data. To tackle this issue, this work introduces a new analytical approach using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) algorithms for sentiment analysis. LSTM models can handle sequential and contextual data well, making them ideal for capturing the flow, tone, and deeper meanings in student responses. This helps us extract relevant insights into student emotions, concerns, and trends over time. By using LSTM, we move beyond superficial evaluations, leading to a better understanding of student sentiment. This helps educators and institutional leaders pinpoint areas for improvement and reinforce successful strategies. Besides LSTM, we also use a Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) model to provide dynamic, personalized suggestions that support individual student development. GPT’s language capabilities allow it to generate constructive, context-aware recommendations based on each student's feedback. By combining these two machine learning techniques, our system analyses student feedback with greater depth and accuracy while offering actionable, individualized recommendations. This intelligent approach improves both student outcomes and institutional effectiveness, making feedback a key tool in modern education.

title: A Knowledge Graph and Large Language Model Approach for Detecting Overclaims in Halal Certification
authors: Ganendra Aby Bhamakerti, Nur Aini Rakhmawati
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: Obtaining halal certification can be subject to errors and frauds. This research attempts to detect errors in halal certification applications submitted by business actors by using Knowledge Graph (KG) with Large Language Model (LLM) and is expected to assist the public in determining whether products and food providers with genuine halal certification are free from any indication of error or fraud. This research is conducted by collecting data sources from HalalWave and GoFood sites, with additional data from Google Maps API. Next, the GoFood dataset is preprocessed by dropping unused columns, deleting duplicate rows, dropping non-halal products and restaurants that have non-halal products, joining tables, and creating new ID columns, resulting in two new datasets that are used in the KG construction stage which involves defining the KG schema, such as determining which nodes and relationships need to be created, and then constructing the KG. Then, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is conducted by using LLM and Google Maps API to find alternative restaurants, KG usage as a knowledge source to find similar “Restaurant” nodes, Jaccard Similarity calculation is conducted to determine whether the restaurant is overclaim or not, and confusion matrix-based metrics is used to evaluate the constructed system performance. The results show that the system demonstrated strong detection ability with an accuracy of 0.91, a precision of 0.908, a recall of 1.0, and an F1-Score of 0.952, which indicate the approach is effective at identifying overclaims on halal certification submissions.

title: Significant Impact of Biomarker-Driven OCT Analysis for Improved Detection of Retinal Pathology with Visual Overlays
authors: Uday Arunrao Mande, Prashant Dhotre, Shafi Pathan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: Purpose: Diabetes can lead to complications such as diabetic nephropathy, diabetic neuropathy, and diabetic macular edema (DME). Similar to DME, AMD-related vision loss can be prevented through early detection and prompt treatment. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is the most widely used imaging technique in ophthalmology for the diagnosis these conditions.
